title: HTML + CSS + System Design + UX/UI Bible — 182 Q&A
body:
HTML · CSS · System Design
UX/UI · Mastery Bible

182 interview questions + deep-dive concepts — ace any frontend interview

HTML is the skeleton of every web page. Semantic HTML is the foundation of accessibility, SEO, and maintainable code.

HTML5 Document Structure

Every HTML document follows this structure. Understanding each part is crucial.

<!DOCTYPE html>                <!-- Tells browser: use HTML5 standards mode -->
<html lang="en">                  <!-- Root element. lang= helps screen readers -->
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">          <!-- Character encoding (ALWAYS first) -->
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
  <meta name="description" content="Page description for SEO">
  <title>Page Title</title>        <!-- Shows in tab, critical for SEO -->
  <link rel="stylesheet" href="styles.css">
  <link rel="icon" href="/favicon.ico">
  <link rel="canonical" href="https://example.com/page">
  
  <!-- Open Graph (social sharing) -->
  <meta property="og:title" content="Title">
  <meta property="og:image" content="https://example.com/og.png">
  
  <!-- Preload critical resources -->
  <link rel="preload" href="font.woff2" as="font" crossorigin>
  <link rel="preconnect" href="https://api.example.com">
</head>
<body>
  <header>...</header>
  <main>...</main>
  <footer>...</footer>
  <script src="app.js" defer></script> <!-- defer = load after HTML parsed -->
</body>
</html>
defer vs async: defer loads JS in parallel but executes AFTER HTML is parsed (in order). async loads in parallel and executes immediately when ready (out of order). Use defer for your app bundle, async for analytics scripts.

Semantic HTML — Every Element

Semantic elements describe their meaning, not just appearance. Critical for accessibility and SEO.

Page Structure

<header>    <!-- Site/section header. Can contain nav, logo, search -->
<nav>       <!-- Navigation links. Use aria-label for multiple navs -->
<main>      <!-- Primary content. ONE per page. Skip-link target -->
<section>   <!-- Thematic grouping WITH a heading -->
<article>   <!-- Self-contained content (blog post, comment, card) -->
<aside>     <!-- Tangentially related (sidebar, pull quote, ad) -->
<footer>    <!-- Site/section footer. Copyright, links -->
<figure>    <!-- Self-contained media with caption -->
<figcaption> <!-- Caption for figure -->
<details>   <!-- Expandable/collapsible content (no JS needed!) -->
<summary>   <!-- Visible heading for details -->
<dialog>    <!-- Native modal dialog. showModal() / close() -->
<time>      <!-- Machine-readable date: <time datetime="2026-04-06"> -->
<mark>      <!-- Highlighted/relevant text -->
<abbr>      <!-- Abbreviation: <abbr title="HyperText...">HTML</abbr> -->
<address>   <!-- Contact information for nearest article/body -->
div and span have NO semantic meaning. They're for styling/grouping only. If you can replace a div with a semantic element (article, section, nav), always do it. Screen readers announce semantic elements — they skip divs.

Forms — Complete Reference

<form action="/submit" method="post" novalidate>
  <!-- Text inputs -->
  <label for="email">Email</label>
  <input type="email" id="email" name="email" required 
         placeholder="you@example.com"
         autocomplete="email"
         pattern="[^@]+@[^@]+\.[^@]+">
  
  <!-- ALL input types -->
  <input type="text">       <input type="password">
  <input type="number" min="0" max="100" step="1">
  <input type="tel">        <input type="url">
  <input type="search">     <input type="date">
  <input type="time">       <input type="datetime-local">
  <input type="color">      <input type="range">
  <input type="file" accept="image/*" multiple>
  <input type="hidden" name="csrf" value="token">
  
  <!-- Select, Textarea, Datalist -->
  <select name="country">
    <optgroup label="Europe">
      <option value="uk">United Kingdom</option>
    </optgroup>
  </select>
  
  <textarea rows="4" maxlength="500"></textarea>
  
  <input list="fruits">
  <datalist id="fruits">
    <option value="Apple">
  </datalist>
  
  <!-- Validation attributes -->
  required  minlength  maxlength  min  max  pattern  step
  
  <button type="submit">Send</button>
</form>
Always use <label> with for= matching input id=. Clicking the label focuses the input. This is essential for accessibility AND improves mobile UX (bigger tap target).

Accessibility (a11y) — Critical Knowledge

Accessibility isn't optional — it's required by law in many countries (ADA, EAA, WCAG).

ARIA Attributes

<!-- Role: what the element IS -->
role="button" role="navigation" role="dialog" role="alert"
role="tab" role="tabpanel" role="tablist"
role="progressbar" role="status" role="search"

<!-- State: current condition -->
aria-expanded="true|false"     <!-- dropdown open/closed -->
aria-selected="true"          <!-- tab/option selected -->
aria-hidden="true"            <!-- hidden from screen readers -->
aria-disabled="true"          <!-- not interactive -->
aria-checked="true"           <!-- checkbox/toggle state -->
aria-current="page"           <!-- current page in nav -->

<!-- Properties: relationships -->
aria-label="Close menu"       <!-- accessible name (no visible text) -->
aria-labelledby="heading-id" <!-- references another element as label -->
aria-describedby="help-id"   <!-- references description element -->
aria-live="polite|assertive"  <!-- announce dynamic changes -->
aria-controls="panel-id"     <!-- this controls that element -->

Focus Management

<!-- Skip link (first element in body) -->
<a href="#main" class="skip-link">Skip to content</a>

<!-- tabindex -->
tabindex="0"    <!-- Add to tab order (for custom interactive elements) -->
tabindex="-1"   <!-- Focusable via JS only (for focus management) -->
<!-- NEVER use tabindex > 0 — breaks natural tab order -->

<!-- Focus trap for modals -->
<!-- Trap focus inside dialog: first/last focusable elements -->
<!-- Use inert attribute on background content -->
<div inert>Background content</div>
First rule of ARIA: Don't use ARIA if a native HTML element does the job. <button> is better than <div role='button' tabindex='0'>. Native elements have built-in keyboard handling, focus management, and screen reader support.

HTML APIs You Should Know

Native Dialog

<dialog id="modal">
  <h2>Confirm</h2>
  <p>Are you sure?</p>
  <form method="dialog">  <!-- auto-closes dialog -->
    <button value="cancel">Cancel</button>
    <button value="confirm">OK</button>
  </form>
</dialog>
// modal.showModal(); — opens with backdrop, focus trap, Escape to close
// modal.close(); — closes programmatically
// modal.returnValue — which button was clicked

Other HTML APIs

Popover API: popover attribute + popovertarget — native tooltips/dropdowns without JS. View Transitions API: document.startViewTransition() — smooth page transitions. Intersection Observer: lazy loading, infinite scroll, scroll animations. Web Components: Custom Elements + Shadow DOM + Templates — framework-agnostic components.

SEO Essentials in HTML

Search engines read your HTML to understand your content. Structure matters.

Critical SEO Elements

Title tag: 50-60 chars, unique per page, primary keyword first. Meta description: 150-160 chars, compelling, includes keywords. Heading hierarchy: One h1 per page, h2-h6 in order (never skip levels). Canonical URL: prevents duplicate content penalties. Alt text: describe images for screen readers AND Google Image Search. Structured data: JSON-LD for rich snippets.

<!-- Structured Data (JSON-LD) -->
<script type="application/ld+json">
{{
  "@context": "https://schema.org",
  "@type": "Article",
  "headline": "Article Title",
  "author": {{ "@type": "Person", "name": "Author" }},
  "datePublished": "2026-04-06"
}}
</script>

Images, video, responsive media, and performance optimization.

Images — Complete Guide

<!-- Responsive image with art direction -->
<picture>
  <source media="(min-width: 800px)" srcset="hero-wide.avif" type="image/avif">
  <source media="(min-width: 800px)" srcset="hero-wide.webp" type="image/webp">
  <source srcset="hero-mobile.avif" type="image/avif">
  <source srcset="hero-mobile.webp" type="image/webp">
  <img src="hero-fallback.jpg" alt="Description" 
       width="1200" height="600"
       loading="lazy"
       decoding="async"
       fetchpriority="high">  <!-- for hero/LCP image -->
</picture>

<!-- Responsive srcset (resolution switching) -->
<img srcset="img-320.jpg 320w, img-640.jpg 640w, img-1200.jpg 1200w"
     sizes="(max-width: 640px) 100vw, 50vw"
     src="img-640.jpg" alt="...">
loading='lazy' defers offscreen images. fetchpriority='high' on your LCP (Largest Contentful Paint) image tells the browser to prioritize it. Always set width and height to prevent layout shift (CLS).

Video, Audio & Embeds

<video controls preload="metadata" poster="thumb.jpg"
       width="640" height="360">
  <source src="video.mp4" type="video/mp4">
  <source src="video.webm" type="video/webm">
  <track kind="subtitles" src="subs-en.vtt" srclang="en" label="English" default>
  Your browser doesn't support video.
</video>

<!-- Responsive iframe -->
<div style="aspect-ratio: 16/9">
  <iframe src="..." style="width:100%;height:100%"
          loading="lazy" allow="fullscreen"></iframe>
</div>

Web Components

// Define a custom element
class MyCard extends HTMLElement {{
  constructor() {{
    super();
    this.attachShadow({{ mode: 'open' }});
    this.shadowRoot.innerHTML = `
      <style>:host {{ display: block; border: 1px solid #ccc; padding: 1rem; }}</style>
      <h2><slot name="title"></slot></h2>
      <slot></slot>
    `;
  }}
  static get observedAttributes() {{ return ['variant']; }}
  attributeChangedCallback(name, old, val) {{ /* react to changes */ }}
}}
customElements.define('my-card', MyCard);

// Usage:
// <my-card><span slot="title">Hello</span>Content here</my-card>
Web Components are framework-agnostic. They work in React, Angular, Vue, or plain HTML. Use them for: design system primitives that must work across teams/frameworks, embeddable widgets, micro-frontends.

Tables, lists, and content elements — the building blocks of structured data display.

Tables — Complete Accessible Reference

<table>
  <caption>Quarterly Revenue</caption>  <!-- Visible title, screen reader announcement -->
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th scope="col">Quarter</th>  <!-- scope tells SR this heads a column -->
      <th scope="col">Revenue</th>
      <th scope="col">Growth</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <th scope="row">Q1</th>  <!-- Row header -->
      <td>$1.2M</td>
      <td>+12%</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
  <tfoot>
    <tr><th scope="row">Total</th><td colspan="2">$4.8M</td></tr>
  </tfoot>
</table>
Always use <caption> and scope attributes. Screen readers use these to announce 'Revenue column, Q1 row: $1.2M' — without them, tables are incomprehensible to blind users.

Lists — Ordered, Unordered, Description

<!-- Unordered list (bullets) -->
<ul>
  <li>Item one</li>
  <li>Item two
    <ul><li>Nested item</li></ul>  <!-- Nested list INSIDE li -->
  </li>
</ul>

<!-- Ordered list (numbers) -->
<ol type="1" start="5" reversed>
  <li value="10">Custom numbering</li>
</ol>

<!-- Description list (key-value pairs, glossary, metadata) -->
<dl>
  <dt>HTML</dt>
  <dd>HyperText Markup Language</dd>
  <dt>CSS</dt>
  <dd>Cascading Style Sheets</dd>
</dl>
Use <dl> for FAQ pages, metadata displays, settings panels — any key-value layout. It's more semantic than a table for term/description pairs.

Details & Summary — Native Accordion

<!-- No JavaScript needed! -->
<details>
  <summary>What is your refund policy?</summary>
  <p>Full refund within 30 days of purchase...</p>
</details>

<!-- Exclusive accordion (name attribute, one open at a time) -->
<details name="faq" open>
  <summary>Question 1</summary>
  <p>Answer 1</p>
</details>
<details name="faq">
  <summary>Question 2</summary>
  <p>Answer 2</p>
</details>
The name attribute (2024) creates exclusive accordions — opening one closes others in the same group. Zero JavaScript. Perfect for FAQ pages.

HTML performance, resource hints, and page load optimization.

Resource Hints — Preload, Prefetch, Preconnect

<!-- PRELOAD: download NOW, needed for current page -->
<link rel="preload" href="critical.css" as="style">
<link rel="preload" href="hero.avif" as="image" fetchpriority="high">
<link rel="preload" href="font.woff2" as="font" type="font/woff2" crossorigin>

<!-- PREFETCH: download later, needed for NEXT page -->
<link rel="prefetch" href="/about">
<link rel="prefetch" href="next-page.js" as="script">

<!-- PRECONNECT: establish connection early (DNS + TCP + TLS) -->
<link rel="preconnect" href="https://api.example.com">
<link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.googleapis.com" crossorigin>

<!-- DNS-PREFETCH: only DNS lookup (less than preconnect) -->
<link rel="dns-prefetch" href="https://analytics.example.com">

<!-- MODULEPRELOAD: preload ES modules -->
<link rel="modulepreload" href="app.js">
Priority hints: fetchpriority='high' on your LCP image/above-fold resources. fetchpriority='low' on below-fold images. Browsers already prioritize, but hints override when you know better.

Script Loading Strategies

<!-- BLOCKING (default): stops HTML parsing -->
<script src="app.js"></script>

<!-- DEFER: download in parallel, execute AFTER HTML parsed, IN ORDER -->
<script src="app.js" defer></script>
<!-- Best for: your app bundle, anything that needs DOM ready -->

<!-- ASYNC: download in parallel, execute IMMEDIATELY when ready, NO ORDER -->
<script src="analytics.js" async></script>
<!-- Best for: independent scripts (analytics, ads, widgets) -->

<!-- MODULE: deferred by default, strict mode, import/export -->
<script type="module" src="app.js"></script>

<!-- INLINE critical: theme/font flash prevention -->
<script>
  document.documentElement.dataset.theme =
    localStorage.getItem('theme') || 'system';
</script>
defer scripts execute in order. async scripts execute in download-completion order (unpredictable). If script B depends on script A, use defer on both — async might run B before A.

Meta Tags — Complete Reference

<!-- Essential -->
<meta charset="UTF-8">                          <!-- ALWAYS first -->
<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1">
<title>Page Title (50-60 chars)</title>
<meta name="description" content="150-160 chars">

<!-- SEO -->
<link rel="canonical" href="https://example.com/page">
<meta name="robots" content="index, follow">
<meta name="robots" content="noindex, nofollow">  <!-- block crawling -->

<!-- Open Graph (Facebook, LinkedIn, iMessage) -->
<meta property="og:title" content="Page Title">
<meta property="og:description" content="Description">
<meta property="og:image" content="https://example.com/og.png">
<meta property="og:url" content="https://example.com/page">
<meta property="og:type" content="website">

<!-- Twitter Card -->
<meta name="twitter:card" content="summary_large_image">
<meta name="twitter:title" content="Title">

<!-- Security -->
<meta http-equiv="Content-Security-Policy" content="default-src 'self'">
<meta http-equiv="X-Content-Type-Options" content="nosniff">

<!-- PWA -->
<link rel="manifest" href="/manifest.json">
<meta name="theme-color" content="#6366f1">
<meta name="apple-mobile-web-app-capable" content="yes">

HTML Performance Checklist

1. Minimize render-blocking resources: Inline critical CSS, defer JS, preload fonts.

2. Optimize images: AVIF/WebP, responsive srcset, lazy loading, width/height attributes (prevent CLS), fetchpriority on LCP image.

3. Reduce HTML size: Remove unnecessary wrappers (use semantic elements), minify in production, enable Brotli/gzip compression.

4. Resource hints: Preconnect to API/CDN origins, preload critical resources, prefetch next-page assets.

5. Font optimization: font-display: swap, preload the WOFF2 file, subset fonts (only needed characters), self-host instead of Google Fonts CDN.

6. Third-party scripts: Load with async, defer non-critical (analytics, chat widgets) until after page load. Use loading='lazy' on iframes.

7. Reduce DOM size: Keep under 1,500 elements. Deep nesting hurts performance. Virtualize long lists. Use content-visibility: auto for offscreen sections.

Run Lighthouse on every PR. Set performance budgets: bundle < 200KB gzipped, LCP < 2.5s, CLS < 0.1. Failing the budget blocks the deploy.

Advanced HTML — APIs, Web Components, and modern capabilities.

Intersection Observer API

// Lazy load images when they enter viewport
const observer = new IntersectionObserver((entries) => {{
  entries.forEach(entry => {{
    if (entry.isIntersecting) {{
      const img = entry.target;
      img.src = img.dataset.src;   // Load real image
      observer.unobserve(img);      // Stop observing
    }}
  }});
}}, {{
  root: null,          // viewport
  rootMargin: '200px',  // load 200px before visible
  threshold: 0          // trigger when any pixel visible
}});

document.querySelectorAll('img[data-src]').forEach(img => observer.observe(img));

Other uses: infinite scroll triggers, scroll-based animations, impression tracking (ads), sticky header detection, lazy-loading components.

Resize Observer & Mutation Observer

// Resize Observer — watch element size changes
const ro = new ResizeObserver(entries => {{
  for (const entry of entries) {{
    const {{ width, height }} = entry.contentRect;
    entry.target.classList.toggle('compact', width < 300);
  }}
}});
ro.observe(element);
// Use case: container-query-like behavior in JS

// Mutation Observer — watch DOM changes
const mo = new MutationObserver(mutations => {{
  mutations.forEach(m => {{
    if (m.type === 'childList') console.log('Children changed');
    if (m.type === 'attributes') console.log('Attr changed:', m.attributeName);
  }});
}});
mo.observe(element, {{ childList: true, attributes: true, subtree: true }});

Web Storage & IndexedDB

// localStorage: persists forever, 5-10MB, sync
localStorage.setItem('theme', 'dark');
localStorage.getItem('theme');  // 'dark'
localStorage.removeItem('theme');

// sessionStorage: same API, cleared on tab close

// IndexedDB: large structured data, async, indexes
const request = indexedDB.open('myDB', 1);
request.onupgradeneeded = (e) => {{
  const db = e.target.result;
  const store = db.createObjectStore('users', {{ keyPath: 'id' }});
  store.createIndex('email', 'email', {{ unique: true }});
}};
// Use Dexie.js wrapper for cleaner API

// Cache API: for caching HTTP responses (Service Worker)
const cache = await caches.open('v1');
await cache.put(request, response);
const cached = await cache.match(request);

Drag and Drop API

<!-- Draggable element -->
<div draggable="true"
     ondragstart="event.dataTransfer.setData('text/plain', this.id)">
  Drag me
</div>

<!-- Drop zone -->
<div ondragover="event.preventDefault()"
     ondrop="handleDrop(event)">
  Drop here
</div>

// Modern approach: use libraries
// @dnd-kit (React), SortableJS, or CDK drag-drop (Angular)
// Native API is functional but has many quirks

Every HTML tag a frontend developer must know, organized by category.

Text & Inline Semantics

<p>           <!-- Paragraph -->
<h1> to <h6>  <!-- Headings (one h1 per page, never skip levels) -->
<span>         <!-- Generic inline container (no meaning) -->
<a href>       <!-- Hyperlink (href, target, rel, download) -->
<strong>       <!-- Strong importance (bold + semantic) -->
<em>           <!-- Emphasis (italic + semantic) -->
<b> <i>        <!-- Visual bold/italic ONLY (no semantic meaning) -->
<u>            <!-- Unarticulated annotation (NOT for links!) -->
<s>            <!-- Strikethrough (no longer accurate) -->
<small>        <!-- Fine print, legal text, copyright -->
<sub> <sup>    <!-- Subscript (H₂O) / Superscript (x²) -->
<mark>         <!-- Highlighted text (search results) -->
<del> <ins>    <!-- Tracked deletions/insertions -->
<abbr title>   <!-- Abbreviation with expansion on hover -->
<code>         <!-- Inline code snippet -->
<pre>          <!-- Preformatted text (preserves whitespace) -->
<kbd>          <!-- Keyboard input (Ctrl+C) -->
<samp>         <!-- Sample output from a program -->
<var>          <!-- Variable in math/programming -->
<q>            <!-- Inline quote (auto adds quotation marks) -->
<blockquote>   <!-- Block-level quotation -->
<cite>         <!-- Title of a work (book, film, article) -->
<br>           <!-- Line break (use CSS spacing instead when possible) -->
<wbr>          <!-- Word break opportunity (for long URLs/code) -->
<hr>           <!-- Thematic break (topic change, not just a line) -->
<ruby><rt>     <!-- Pronunciation guide (CJK languages) -->
<bdi><bdo>     <!-- Bidirectional text isolation/override -->
Every one of these has a PURPOSE. Don't use <b> when you mean <strong>. Don't use <br> for spacing (use CSS margin). Don't use <i> when you mean <em>. Semantic elements help screen readers, SEO, and future developers reading your code.

Interactive & Embedded Elements

<button>       <!-- Clickable (type=submit | button | reset) -->
<details>      <!-- Native expandable/collapsible (no JS!) -->
<summary>      <!-- Visible heading for details -->
<dialog>       <!-- Modal/non-modal (.showModal() / .show()) -->
<menu>         <!-- Toolbar or context menu container -->
<output>       <!-- Result of calculation (for= links inputs) -->
<progress>     <!-- Task completion (value/max) -->
<meter>        <!-- Measurement gauge (value/min/max/low/high) -->

MEDIA:
<img>          <!-- Image (ALWAYS include alt, width, height) -->
<picture>      <!-- Responsive image (art direction + formats) -->
<video>        <!-- Video (controls, poster, preload, autoplay) -->
<audio>        <!-- Audio player -->
<source>       <!-- Media source (inside picture/video/audio) -->
<track>        <!-- Subtitles/captions for video/audio -->
<canvas>       <!-- 2D/WebGL drawing surface -->
<svg>          <!-- Scalable vector graphics -->
<iframe>       <!-- Embed another page (sandbox for security) -->
<embed>        <!-- Embed external content -->
<object>       <!-- Embed with fallback content -->
<map><area>    <!-- Image map with clickable regions -->

SCRIPTING:
<script>       <!-- JavaScript (defer | async | type=module) -->
<noscript>     <!-- Fallback when JS disabled -->
<template>     <!-- Inert template (not rendered until cloned) -->
<slot>         <!-- Insertion point in Shadow DOM -->

Form Elements — Complete

<form>         <!-- Form container (action, method, novalidate) -->
<input>        <!-- 22 types: text, email, password, number, tel,
                    url, search, date, time, datetime-local,
                    month, week, color, range, file, hidden,
                    checkbox, radio, submit, reset, button, image -->
<textarea>     <!-- Multi-line text (rows, cols, maxlength) -->
<select>       <!-- Dropdown (multiple for multi-select) -->
<optgroup>     <!-- Group options with label -->
<option>       <!-- Select option (value, selected, disabled) -->
<label>        <!-- Label (for= MUST match input id) -->
<fieldset>     <!-- Group related fields -->
<legend>       <!-- Fieldset caption -->
<datalist>     <!-- Autocomplete suggestions for input -->
<button>       <!-- Submit/reset/button (prefer over input type=submit) -->

VALIDATION ATTRIBUTES:
required       <!-- Must be filled -->
minlength/maxlength  <!-- Text length -->
min/max/step   <!-- Number/date range -->
pattern        <!-- Regex validation -->
autocomplete   <!-- Browser autofill hint (email, name, tel, etc.) -->
inputmode      <!-- Virtual keyboard type (numeric, tel, email) -->
enterkeyhint   <!-- Mobile Enter key label (search, send, go) -->
Always use <button type="button"> for non-submit buttons inside forms. Without type="button", buttons default to type="submit" and will submit the form when clicked!

Structural & Semantic — Complete Map

DOCUMENT:
<html>         <!-- Root (lang= for screen readers + SEO) -->
<head>         <!-- Metadata container -->
<body>         <!-- Visible content -->
<title>        <!-- Page title (50-60 chars, unique per page) -->
<meta>         <!-- Metadata (charset, viewport, description, og:*) -->
<link>         <!-- External resource (CSS, favicon, preload) -->
<style>        <!-- Embedded CSS -->
<base>         <!-- Base URL for relative links (rarely used) -->

SEMANTIC STRUCTURE:
<header>       <!-- Page/section header (logo, nav, search) -->
<nav>          <!-- Navigation links (aria-label if multiple) -->
<main>         <!-- Primary content (ONE per page) -->
<section>      <!-- Thematic grouping WITH a heading -->
<article>      <!-- Self-contained content (blog post, card) -->
<aside>        <!-- Tangentially related (sidebar, ad) -->
<footer>       <!-- Page/section footer (copyright, links) -->
<figure>       <!-- Self-contained media with caption -->
<figcaption>   <!-- Caption for figure -->
<address>      <!-- Contact info for nearest article/body -->
<time>         <!-- Machine-readable date (datetime= attribute) -->
<search>       <!-- Search form container (NEW in 2023) -->
<hgroup>       <!-- Group heading + subtitle -->

LISTS:
<ul>           <!-- Unordered list -->
<ol>           <!-- Ordered list (type, start, reversed) -->
<li>           <!-- List item -->
<dl>           <!-- Description list (glossary, FAQ, metadata) -->
<dt>           <!-- Description term -->
<dd>           <!-- Description detail -->

TABLE:
<table>        <!-- Data table (NOT for layout!) -->
<caption>      <!-- Table title (screen reader announced) -->
<thead>        <!-- Header rows -->
<tbody>        <!-- Body rows -->
<tfoot>        <!-- Footer rows (totals) -->
<tr>           <!-- Table row -->
<th>           <!-- Header cell (scope=col|row) -->
<td>           <!-- Data cell (colspan, rowspan) -->
<colgroup><col> <!-- Column styling -->
Never use <table> for page layout — that's a 1990s practice. Tables are ONLY for tabular data. Use CSS Grid and Flexbox for layout. Screen readers announce tables as data grids, which confuses users when it's actually a page layout.

52 interview questions from very basic to very advanced. Click to reveal answers.

Showing 52 questions
BWhat is HTML?+

HyperText Markup Language — the standard markup language for web pages. It defines the structure and content of a page using elements (tags). HTML is NOT a programming language — it's a markup language that describes document structure.

BWhat is the DOCTYPE declaration?+

<!DOCTYPE html> tells the browser to use HTML5 standards mode (not quirks mode). Must be the FIRST line. Without it, browsers may render in quirks mode — inconsistent behavior across browsers.

BWhat are semantic HTML elements?+

Elements that describe their meaning: <header>, <nav>, <main>, <article>, <section>, <aside>, <footer>, <figure>, <time>, <mark>. They improve accessibility (screen readers announce them), SEO (search engines understand page structure), and code readability.

BWhat is the difference between div and span?+

<div> is a block-level generic container (takes full width, starts new line). <span> is an inline generic container (only as wide as content, stays in flow). Neither has semantic meaning — use them only when no semantic element fits.

BWhat is the difference between block and inline elements?+

Block: takes full width, starts new line, can contain block/inline elements (div, p, h1-h6, section). Inline: only as wide as content, stays in text flow, can only contain inline elements (span, a, strong, em, img). Inline-block: inline flow but accepts width/height.

BWhat is the alt attribute on images?+

Alternative text displayed when image can't load and announced by screen readers. Should describe the image content. Decorative images: alt="" (empty, not missing). Missing alt is an accessibility violation. Also helps SEO (Google Image search).

BWhat is the difference between id and class?+

id: unique per page (one element), high specificity, used for anchors (href="#section") and JS targeting. class: reusable across many elements, lower specificity, used for styling. One element can have multiple classes but only one id.

BWhat are meta tags?+

Provide metadata about the page in <head>. charset: character encoding. viewport: mobile responsiveness. description: SEO snippet. robots: crawling instructions. og:*: social sharing preview. They don't appear visually but affect browser behavior and SEO.

BWhat is the difference between defer and async on script tags?+

defer: downloads in parallel with HTML parsing, executes AFTER HTML is fully parsed, maintains order. async: downloads in parallel, executes IMMEDIATELY when ready (may interrupt parsing), no guaranteed order. Use defer for your app code, async for independent scripts (analytics).

BWhat is localStorage vs sessionStorage vs cookies?+

localStorage: 5-10MB, persists forever, same-origin, JS only. sessionStorage: 5MB, cleared on tab close, per-tab. Cookies: 4KB, sent with every HTTP request, server-accessible (httpOnly), expiration control. Use cookies for auth tokens (httpOnly), localStorage for preferences, sessionStorage for temporary data.

IWhat is the difference between article and section?+

<article>: self-contained, independently distributable content (could be extracted and still make sense). Blog post, comment, product card. <section>: thematic grouping of content WITH a heading. Part of a larger piece. Use section for chapters, article for standalone pieces.

IExplain the native dialog element.+

<dialog> provides native modal/non-modal dialogs. .showModal() opens with backdrop, focus trap, Escape-to-close. .close() closes. method="dialog" on forms auto-closes. ::backdrop pseudo-element for overlay. Returns .returnValue. Eliminates need for custom modal libraries in many cases.

IWhat is the Popover API?+

Native popover/tooltip behavior: <button popovertarget="menu"> + <div id="menu" popover>. Auto: light-dismiss (click outside closes). Manual: explicit open/close only. Handles: positioning, focus management, stacking, Escape key. Reduces JS for dropdowns, menus, tooltips.

IWhat are Web Components?+

Custom HTML elements with encapsulated markup/styles/behavior. Three technologies: 1) Custom Elements: define new tags with customElements.define(). 2) Shadow DOM: encapsulated DOM tree (styles don't leak in/out). 3) HTML Templates: <template> and <slot> for reusable markup. Framework-agnostic — work everywhere.

IWhat is the Content Security Policy (CSP)?+

HTTP header that controls what resources can load: Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' cdn.example.com; style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'. Prevents XSS by blocking inline scripts, unauthorized domains. nonce attribute allows specific inline scripts. Essential security header.

IExplain srcset and sizes for responsive images.+

srcset provides multiple image versions: srcset="small.jpg 320w, medium.jpg 640w, large.jpg 1200w". sizes tells browser how wide the image will display: sizes="(max-width: 640px) 100vw, 50vw". Browser picks the best file based on viewport + DPR. The <picture> element adds art direction (different crops per breakpoint).

IWhat is the fetchpriority attribute?+

fetchpriority="high" on images tells the browser to prioritize downloading this resource. Use on LCP (hero) images. fetchpriority="low" for below-fold images. Works on <img>, <link>, <script>, and fetch(). Complements loading="lazy" for fine-grained resource control.

IWhat are data attributes?+

Custom attributes prefixed with data-: <div data-user-id="123" data-role="admin">. Access in JS: element.dataset.userId (camelCase). Access in CSS: [data-role="admin"] { }. Use for: passing data to JS, styling hooks, test selectors. Don't store sensitive data (visible in source).

IWhat is ARIA live region?+

aria-live="polite|assertive" announces dynamic content changes to screen readers. polite: announces when user is idle (toast notifications). assertive: interrupts immediately (error messages). Add to a container, then update its content — screen reader announces the change. Essential for SPAs where content updates without page reload.

AHow does the browser render a page? (Critical Rendering Path)+

1) Parse HTML → DOM tree. 2) Parse CSS → CSSOM. 3) Combine DOM + CSSOM → Render tree (visible elements only). 4) Layout: calculate geometry (position, size) of every element. 5) Paint: fill pixels (text, colors, images, borders). 6) Composite: combine layers (GPU). CSS blocks rendering. JS blocks HTML parsing. Preload scanner reads ahead. Understanding this pipeline is key to performance optimization.

AExplain the Shadow DOM in depth.+

An encapsulated DOM tree attached to an element. Styles inside don't leak out, external styles don't leak in. Created with element.attachShadow({mode:'open'}). open: accessible via .shadowRoot. closed: not accessible (rare). <slot> projects child content (like ng-content/children). CSS ::part() allows external styling of specific parts. Foundation of Web Components and used by native elements (input, select).

AWhat is the View Transitions API?+

document.startViewTransition(() => updateDOM()) creates smooth transitions between DOM states. The browser: 1) Screenshots old state. 2) Runs your DOM update. 3) Screenshots new state. 4) Cross-fades between them. CSS pseudo-elements ::view-transition-old and ::view-transition-new for custom animations. Works with MPA navigations in Chromium. Eliminates need for complex JS animation libraries for page transitions.

AWhat are the differences between Service Workers, Web Workers, and Shared Workers?+

Web Worker: Background thread for heavy computation. Private to one tab. new Worker(). Shared Worker: Shared across all tabs of same origin. new SharedWorker(). Communication via ports. Service Worker: Proxy between app and network. Intercepts fetch requests for offline caching, push notifications, background sync. Registered with navigator.serviceWorker.register(). Has lifecycle (install, activate, fetch events).

AExplain the Intersection Observer API.+

Asynchronously observes visibility of elements relative to viewport or ancestor. new IntersectionObserver(callback, { root, rootMargin, threshold }). callback receives entries with isIntersecting, intersectionRatio. Uses: lazy loading images, infinite scroll triggers, scroll-based animations, impression tracking, sticky header detection. Replaces scroll event listeners (much more performant).

AHow do you implement an accessible modal?+

1) Use <dialog> element with .showModal(). 2) Focus trap: first focusable element gets focus on open, Tab cycles within modal. 3) aria-modal="true", role="dialog", aria-labelledby pointing to heading. 4) Close on Escape key. 5) inert attribute on background content. 6) Return focus to trigger element on close. 7) Prevent body scroll. The native dialog handles most of this automatically.

BWhat is the difference between section, article, and div?+

section: thematic grouping WITH a heading (chapters, tabbed content). article: self-contained content that makes sense independently (blog post, comment, product card). div: no semantic meaning, just a generic container for styling/scripting.

BWhat is the purpose of the head element?+

Contains metadata about the document — not displayed to users. Includes: <title>, <meta> (charset, viewport, description), <link> (stylesheets, favicon, preload), <script>, <style>, Open Graph tags, and structured data.

BWhat is the viewport meta tag?+

<meta name='viewport' content='width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0'> tells mobile browsers to: set the viewport width to the device width (not a default 980px), set initial zoom to 100%. Without it, mobile browsers render a desktop-width page scaled down. Essential for responsive design.

BWhat are void/self-closing elements?+

Elements that cannot have children and don't need closing tags: <img>, <input>, <br>, <hr>, <meta>, <link>, <source>, <area>, <col>, <embed>, <wbr>. The trailing slash (<img />) is optional in HTML5 but required in JSX/React.

BWhat is the difference between strong/em and b/i?+

<strong>: semantic importance (screen readers emphasize). <b>: visually bold, no semantic meaning. <em>: semantic emphasis (screen readers change tone). <i>: visually italic, no emphasis. Prefer strong/em for meaning, b/i only for stylistic purposes.

BWhat is the target attribute on anchor tags?+

_self: same tab (default). _blank: new tab/window (add rel='noopener noreferrer' for security). _parent: parent frame. _top: full window. _blank without noopener gives the new page access to window.opener (security risk).

IWhat is the picture element?+

Container for multiple <source> elements for responsive images. Enables: art direction (different crops per screen size), format selection (AVIF → WebP → JPEG fallback), and media-query-based image switching. The browser picks the first matching source. The <img> inside is the fallback.

IWhat is the difference between preload, prefetch, and preconnect?+

preload: download this resource NOW, it's needed for current page (fonts, critical CSS/JS). prefetch: download this resource later, it's needed for NEXT navigation (next page's JS). preconnect: establish connection to origin early (DNS + TCP + TLS). dns-prefetch: only DNS lookup.

IWhat is the inert attribute?+

Makes an element and all descendants non-interactive and invisible to assistive tech. <div inert>...</div>. Use for: background content when modal is open, disabled sections. The element is not focusable, not clickable, not announced by screen readers. Much better than aria-hidden + tabindex=-1 on every child.

IWhat are ARIA landmarks?+

Regions of a page that assistive tech can navigate to directly: banner (header), navigation (nav), main (main), complementary (aside), contentinfo (footer), search, form. Semantic HTML elements automatically create landmarks: <header> = banner, <nav> = navigation. Screen readers let users jump between landmarks with keyboard shortcuts.

IWhat is progressive enhancement?+

Build a baseline functional experience for all browsers/devices, then enhance with advanced features for capable ones. HTML (structure) → CSS (presentation) → JS (interactivity). Example: a form works with plain HTML (submit to server), enhanced with JS (AJAX submit, validation, animations). Opposite of graceful degradation.

AWhat is the Mutation Observer API?+

Watches for changes to the DOM tree: additions/removals of child elements, attribute changes, text content changes. new MutationObserver(callback).observe(target, { childList: true, attributes: true, subtree: true }). Replaces deprecated Mutation Events. Uses: detecting third-party DOM changes, auto-initializing widgets, content change tracking. More performant than polling.

AWhat is the Performance API?+

Measures page and resource performance. performance.now(): high-resolution timestamp. performance.mark('start') + performance.measure('duration', 'start'): custom timing. PerformanceObserver: observe LCP, FID, CLS, Long Tasks. performance.getEntriesByType('resource'): all resource load times. Foundation for Real User Monitoring (RUM).

AWhat is the difference between Canvas and SVG?+

Canvas: Bitmap/raster, immediate mode (draw and forget), pixel manipulation, better for: complex scenes, games, image processing, many objects. SVG: Vector, retained mode (DOM elements), scalable without blur, accessible (text in SVG is readable), better for: icons, charts, logos, animations, interactive graphics. Canvas: JS-driven. SVG: CSS-animatable, DOM-queryable.

AWhat are Web Vitals and how do you measure them in code?+

Use the web-vitals library: import {onLCP, onINP, onCLS} from 'web-vitals'. Each function takes a callback with the metric value. Send to analytics. PerformanceObserver for custom measurement: new PerformanceObserver(list => list.getEntries().forEach(entry => ...)) with type: 'largest-contentful-paint'. Automate with Lighthouse CI in your pipeline.

BWhat is the difference between link and @import for CSS?+

<link> in HTML: loads CSS in parallel, non-blocking (with media attribute), recommended. @import in CSS: sequential loading (blocks), can't be conditional, creates waterfall. Always use <link> — @import adds extra round trips and slows rendering.

BWhat is the autocomplete attribute?+

Tells browser what data to auto-fill: autocomplete='email', 'name', 'tel', 'address-line1', 'cc-number', 'new-password', 'current-password'. Massive UX win — users fill forms 30% faster. Required for WCAG 1.3.5 compliance. Set autocomplete='off' to disable (use sparingly).

BWhat is the difference between absolute and relative URL paths?+

Absolute: https://example.com/page — full URL. Protocol-relative: //example.com/page — matches current protocol (deprecated). Root-relative: /page — relative to domain root. Relative: ./page or ../page — relative to current file. Use root-relative for internal links, absolute for external.

IWhat is the loading attribute?+

loading='lazy' defers loading until near viewport (images, iframes). loading='eager' loads immediately (default). Use lazy for below-fold content, eager (or omit) for above-fold. Works natively in all modern browsers — no IntersectionObserver needed for basic lazy loading. Combine with fetchpriority for fine control.

IWhat is the dialog element and how does it differ from a div modal?+

<dialog> provides: native backdrop (::backdrop), focus trapping (Tab stays inside), Escape to close, inert on background, proper stacking above all content. A <div> modal needs ALL of this manually — focus trap, scroll lock, Escape handler, backdrop, z-index battles. Use native dialog and save 100+ lines of JS.

IWhat is the contenteditable attribute?+

contenteditable='true' makes any element editable by the user. Used as the foundation for rich text editors (with execCommand or modern document.execCommand replacement). Gotchas: inconsistent behavior across browsers, XSS risk if saving user input, difficult to control formatting. Modern editors use it with sanitization layers.

IWhat are CORS and how does it relate to HTML?+

Cross-Origin Resource Sharing affects HTML loading: crossorigin attribute on <script>, <link>, <img> controls CORS mode. crossorigin='anonymous': no credentials sent. crossorigin='use-credentials': cookies sent. Required for: preloaded fonts, canvas tainted images, error details for cross-origin scripts.

IWhat is the picture element vs img srcset?+

<img srcset>: resolution switching — browser picks best size for same image. <picture>: art direction — serve different images per breakpoint (different crops, orientations). Also for format fallback: <source type='image/avif'> → <source type='image/webp'> → <img src='fallback.jpg'>.

AHow does the browser's preload scanner work?+

When the main HTML parser is blocked (by CSS or sync JS), a lightweight secondary scanner reads ahead to discover resources (images, scripts, stylesheets) and starts downloading them in parallel. This is why <link rel='stylesheet'> in the head doesn't completely block resource discovery. It's also why dynamically injected scripts miss this optimization — add resource hints (preload) for critical dynamic resources.

AWhat is the Speculation Rules API?+

Declarative prefetching/prerendering for faster navigation: <script type='speculationrules'>{"prerender":[{"where":{"href_matches":"/blog/*"}}]}</script>. The browser preloads or even fully renders pages the user might navigate to next. More efficient than <link rel='prefetch'> because it uses document rules and eagerness levels. Supported in Chromium browsers.

AWhat is declarative Shadow DOM?+

<template shadowrootmode='open'> creates Shadow DOM without JavaScript — works with SSR. The server sends the shadow root as HTML, the browser attaches it as a shadow tree. Enables Web Components to be server-rendered. Without it, Web Components were client-only (required JS to attachShadow()).

AHow do you implement a skip link?+

<a href='#main' class='skip-link'>Skip to content</a> — first element in body, visually hidden until focused, jumps to <main id='main'>. CSS: .skip-link { position: absolute; transform: translateY(-100%); } .skip-link:focus { transform: translateY(0); }. Essential for keyboard users to bypass repetitive navigation. WCAG 2.4.1 requirement.

CSS controls how everything looks. Master the box model, layout, and modern features.

The Box Model — Foundation of Everything

/* Every element is a box with 4 layers: */
/* content → padding → border → margin */

/* DEFAULT (content-box): width = content only */
.box {{ width: 200px; padding: 20px; border: 2px solid; }}
/* Actual width = 200 + 20*2 + 2*2 = 244px 😫 */

/* BORDER-BOX: width includes padding + border */
*, *::before, *::after {{ box-sizing: border-box; }}
.box {{ width: 200px; padding: 20px; border: 2px solid; }}
/* Actual width = 200px ✅ (content shrinks to fit) */

/* Margin collapsing — margins DON'T add, they overlap */
.a {{ margin-bottom: 20px; }}
.b {{ margin-top: 30px; }}
/* Gap between a and b = 30px (not 50px!) */
/* Collapsing happens only vertically, not in flex/grid */
Always set box-sizing: border-box globally. It's the first line in every modern CSS reset. Without it, padding/border calculations will constantly surprise you.

Flexbox — Complete Reference

/* Container properties */
.flex {{ 
  display: flex;
  flex-direction: row | column | row-reverse | column-reverse;
  justify-content: flex-start | center | flex-end | space-between | space-around | space-evenly;
  align-items: stretch | flex-start | center | flex-end | baseline;
  flex-wrap: nowrap | wrap | wrap-reverse;
  gap: 1rem;              /* gap between items */
  align-content: stretch | center | space-between; /* multi-line */
}}

/* Item properties */
.item {{
  flex: 1;              /* shorthand: flex-grow flex-shrink flex-basis */
  flex-grow: 1;          /* how much to grow (0 = don't) */
  flex-shrink: 0;         /* how much to shrink (0 = don't) */
  flex-basis: 200px;      /* starting size before grow/shrink */
  align-self: center;     /* override align-items for this item */
  order: -1;             /* visual reorder (don't use for a11y) */
}}

/* Common patterns */
/* Center anything */
.center {{ display: flex; justify-content: center; align-items: center; }}

/* Sticky footer */
body {{ display: flex; flex-direction: column; min-height: 100vh; }}
main {{ flex: 1; }}

/* Space between with last item pushed right */
.nav {{ display: flex; gap: 1rem; }}
.nav .logout {{ margin-left: auto; }}
justify-content = alignment along the MAIN axis (horizontal in row, vertical in column). align-items = alignment along the CROSS axis (vertical in row, horizontal in column). Think: justify = main, align = cross.

CSS Grid — Complete Reference

/* Container */
.grid {{
  display: grid;
  grid-template-columns: 1fr 2fr 1fr;          /* 3 columns */
  grid-template-columns: repeat(3, 1fr);       /* same thing */
  grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fill, minmax(250px, 1fr)); /* responsive! */
  grid-template-rows: auto 1fr auto;
  gap: 1rem;
  
  /* Named areas */
  grid-template-areas:
    "header header header"
    "sidebar main   aside"
    "footer footer  footer";
}}

/* Items */
.header {{ grid-area: header; }}
.sidebar {{ grid-area: sidebar; }}
.main {{ grid-area: main; }}

/* Spanning */
.hero {{ grid-column: 1 / -1; }}     /* full width */
.wide {{ grid-column: span 2; }}      /* span 2 columns */

/* Auto-placement */
.masonry {{ 
  grid-template-columns: repeat(3, 1fr);
  grid-auto-rows: minmax(100px, auto);
}}

/* Subgrid (child inherits parent's grid lines) */
.child {{ 
  display: grid;
  grid-template-columns: subgrid; /* align with parent's columns */
}}
repeat(auto-fill, minmax(250px, 1fr)) is the holy grail of responsive layouts — creates as many 250px+ columns as fit, no media queries needed. auto-fit is similar but collapses empty tracks.

Positioning

/* static — default, normal flow */
/* relative — offset from normal position, creates stacking context */
/* absolute — removed from flow, positioned to nearest positioned ancestor */
/* fixed — positioned to viewport (stays on scroll) */
/* sticky — hybrid: normal until scroll threshold, then fixed */

.sticky-nav {{
  position: sticky;
  top: 0;              /* sticks when scrolled to top */
  z-index: 100;
}}

.modal-overlay {{
  position: fixed;
  inset: 0;            /* shorthand for top/right/bottom/left: 0 */
  z-index: 1000;
}}

.tooltip {{
  position: absolute;
  top: 100%;           /* below the parent */
  left: 50%;
  transform: translateX(-50%); /* center horizontally */
}}
BWhat is the difference between strong/em and b/i?+

<strong>: semantic importance (screen readers emphasize). <b>: bold, no meaning. <em>: emphasis (screen readers change tone). <i>: italic, no meaning. Also: <small> = fine print, <s> = no longer accurate, <del>/<ins> = tracked changes, <mark> = highlighted.

BWhat are global HTML attributes?+

Valid on ANY element: id, class, style, title (tooltip), tabindex, hidden, draggable, contenteditable, dir, lang, data-*, role, aria-*, slot, part, translate, spellcheck, autofocus, enterkeyhint, inputmode, popover, inert, nonce. Know these — they're asked frequently.

BWhat heading levels should you use and why?+

One <h1> per page (main title, critical for SEO). <h2-h6> in order — never skip levels. Screen readers let users jump between headings. Search engines use the hierarchy. Use CSS to control visual size — don't pick heading level based on appearance.

BWhat is the difference between meter and progress?+

<progress value=70 max=100>: task completion (upload, loading). <meter value=0.7 min=0 max=1 low=0.3 high=0.8>: measurement within a range (disk usage, score). Progress = something happening over time. Meter = static gauge.

BWhat are the anchor tag's key attributes?+

href (URL, #hash, mailto:, tel:), target (_self, _blank), rel (noopener, noreferrer, nofollow), download (triggers download), hreflang (linked page language). For _blank, always add rel="noopener" — without it, the new page can access window.opener (security risk).

IWhat is the Popover API?+

<button popovertarget="id"> + <div popover>. Native show/hide with: light-dismiss (click outside closes), Escape key, focus management, top-layer rendering. auto = light-dismiss, manual = explicit only. Replaces many dropdown/tooltip JS libraries. Supported in all modern browsers since 2024.

IWhat is the hidden="until-found" attribute?+

Content is hidden but findable by browser find-in-page (Ctrl+F) and anchor links. When found, it automatically reveals. Perfect for collapsed/accordion content that should remain searchable. Regular hidden makes content completely invisible to find-in-page.

IWhat is the sandbox attribute on iframes?+

Restricts iframe capabilities: sandbox="allow-scripts allow-same-origin". Without values, ALL restrictions apply (no scripts, forms, popups, navigation, plugins). Add permissions back as needed. Essential for securely embedding untrusted third-party content.

IWhat is fieldset/legend and when do you use them?+

<fieldset> + <legend> groups related form controls with a semantic label. Screen readers announce: "Contact Information group: Name field, Email field." A plain div has no grouping semantics. Use for: radio button groups, checkbox groups, address sections, any cluster of related fields.

AWhat are all input types and their mobile behaviors?+

email: @ key. tel: phone dialer. url: / and .com. number: number pad (NOT for phone — use tel). search: search icon. date/time/datetime-local: native pickers. color: color picker. range: slider. file: file browser. Tip: inputmode="numeric" on text input gives number keyboard WITHOUT the spinner arrows of type=number.

AWhat is the Sanitizer API?+

Browser-native HTML sanitization: removes dangerous elements (script, iframe) and attributes (onclick, onerror) while preserving safe content. Replaces DOMPurify. Configurable allow/deny lists. Not yet widely supported but the future of XSS prevention for user-generated content displayed via innerHTML.

Modern CSS features that have changed how we write styles in 2025-2026.

CSS Custom Properties (Variables)

/* Define on :root for global access */
:root {{
  --color-primary: #6366f1;
  --color-text: #1f2937;
  --spacing: 1rem;
  --radius: 8px;
  --shadow: 0 4px 12px rgb(0 0 0 / 0.1);
}}

/* Dark mode with variables */
@media (prefers-color-scheme: dark) {{
  :root {{
    --color-primary: #818cf8;
    --color-text: #e5e7eb;
  }}
}}

/* Component-scoped overrides */
.card {{ --card-padding: 1.5rem; padding: var(--card-padding); }}
.card.compact {{ --card-padding: 0.75rem; }}

/* With fallbacks */
color: var(--undefined-var, #000);

Container Queries

/* Parent defines a container */
.card-grid {{
  container-type: inline-size;
  container-name: card-grid;
}}

/* Children respond to CONTAINER width, not viewport */
@container card-grid (min-width: 600px) {{
  .card {{ flex-direction: row; }}
}}

@container (min-width: 400px) {{
  .card-title {{ font-size: 1.5rem; }}
}}

/* Container query units */
.text {{ font-size: 3cqi; }} /* 3% of container inline size */
Media queries ask 'how wide is the screen?' Container queries ask 'how wide is my parent?' This is revolutionary for reusable components — a card can adapt whether it's in a sidebar or main content area.

Modern Selectors & At-Rules

/* :has() — the parent selector (game changer!) */
.card:has(img) {{ grid-template-rows: auto 1fr; }}  /* only if card has image */
.form:has(:invalid) .submit {{ opacity: 0.5; }}  /* dim submit if form invalid */
h2:has(+ p) {{ margin-bottom: 0.5rem; }}           /* h2 followed by p */

/* :is() and :where() — group selectors */
:is(h1, h2, h3) {{ font-family: serif; }}
/* :where() is same but zero specificity */

/* Nesting (native CSS, no Sass needed!) */
.card {{
  padding: 1rem;
  & .title {{ font-size: 1.5rem; }}
  &:hover {{ background: #f5f5f5; }}
  & > .body {{ flex: 1; }}
  @media (width >= 768px) {{ padding: 2rem; }}
}}

/* @layer — control cascade order */
@layer base, components, utilities;
@layer base {{ a {{ color: blue; }} }}
@layer utilities {{ .text-red {{ color: red; }} }}

/* @scope — scoped styles */
@scope (.card) to (.card-footer) {{
  p {{ color: gray; }} /* only paragraphs between .card and .card-footer */
}}
:has() is the most powerful CSS selector ever added. It lets you style a parent based on its children — something that was impossible for 25 years. It enables things that previously required JavaScript.

Animations & Transitions

/* Transitions — smooth state changes */
.btn {{
  transition: all 0.2s ease;
  /* Better: only transition what changes */
  transition: background-color 0.2s, transform 0.15s;
}}
.btn:hover {{ background: blue; transform: translateY(-2px); }}

/* Keyframe animations */
@keyframes slide-in {{
  from {{ transform: translateX(-100%); opacity: 0; }}
  to {{ transform: translateX(0); opacity: 1; }}
}}
.panel {{ animation: slide-in 0.3s ease-out forwards; }}

/* View Transitions API (page transitions) */
::view-transition-old(root) {{ animation: fade-out 0.2s; }}
::view-transition-new(root) {{ animation: fade-in 0.2s; }}

/* Scroll-driven animations (no JS!) */
@keyframes reveal {{ from {{ opacity: 0; }} to {{ opacity: 1; }} }}
.card {{
  animation: reveal linear;
  animation-timeline: view();
  animation-range: entry 0% cover 40%;
}}

/* Respect user preferences */
@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) {{
  *, *::before, *::after {{ 
    animation-duration: 0.01ms !important;
    transition-duration: 0.01ms !important;
  }}
}}

Responsive Design — Complete Toolkit

/* Mobile-first media queries */
.grid {{ display: grid; grid-template-columns: 1fr; }}

@media (min-width: 640px) {{ /* sm */
  .grid {{ grid-template-columns: repeat(2, 1fr); }}
}}
@media (min-width: 1024px) {{ /* lg */
  .grid {{ grid-template-columns: repeat(3, 1fr); }}
}}

/* Modern: no breakpoints needed */
.grid {{ grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(min(300px, 100%), 1fr)); }}

/* Fluid typography (clamp) */
h1 {{ font-size: clamp(1.5rem, 4vw, 3rem); }}
/* min: 1.5rem, preferred: 4vw, max: 3rem */

/* Logical properties (LTR/RTL support) */
.card {{
  margin-inline: auto;        /* margin-left + margin-right */
  padding-block: 1rem;        /* padding-top + padding-bottom */
  border-inline-start: 3px solid blue; /* left in LTR, right in RTL */
  inset-inline-start: 0;     /* left in LTR, right in RTL */
}}

CSS architecture, methodologies, performance, and the modern tooling landscape.

CSS Specificity — The Rules

/* Specificity: (inline, IDs, classes, elements) */
*                    /* 0,0,0,0 */
p                    /* 0,0,0,1 */
.class               /* 0,0,1,0 */
#id                  /* 0,1,0,0 */
style="..."          /* 1,0,0,0 */
!important           /* overrides everything (avoid!) */

p.intro              /* 0,0,1,1 (element + class) */

/* :where() has ZERO specificity */

/* :is() takes HIGHEST specificity of its arguments */

/* @layer lowers entire layer priority */
@layer base {{ #id {{ color: red; }} }} /* loses to un-layered .class */
Specificity is NOT a single number. It's a tuple: (inline, IDs, classes, elements). (0,1,0,0) ALWAYS beats (0,0,99,99) — one ID beats 99 classes. This is why !important and inline styles are hard to override.

Performance Optimization

Critical Rendering Path

HTML parsing → DOM tree + CSSOM → Render tree → Layout → Paint → Composite. CSS blocks rendering — the browser won't paint until ALL CSS is parsed. JS blocks HTML parsing (unless defer/async).

Optimization Techniques

Critical CSS: Inline above-fold styles in <head>, lazy-load the rest. content-visibility: auto: Skip rendering offscreen content. contain: Isolate layout/paint/style recalculation. will-change: Hint browser about upcoming animations (use sparingly). Avoid layout thrashing: batch reads before writes. Prefer transform/opacity: Only these two properties are GPU-composited — avoid animating width/height/top/left.

/* content-visibility: skip rendering offscreen sections */
.section {{ content-visibility: auto; contain-intrinsic-size: auto 500px; }}

/* contain: isolate recalculation */
.card {{ contain: layout style paint; }}

/* Only animate compositable properties */
.animate {{ 
  transform: translateX(0);  /* ✅ GPU composited */
  opacity: 1;                /* ✅ GPU composited */
  /* ❌ Avoid: width, height, top, left, margin, padding */
}}

Showing 60 questions
BWhat is the CSS box model?+

Every element is a rectangular box with: content (inner area) → padding (space inside border) → border → margin (space outside border). box-sizing: border-box makes width/height include padding+border (much more intuitive than the default content-box).

BWhat is the difference between margin and padding?+

Padding: space INSIDE the border (between content and border). Affected by background-color. Margin: space OUTSIDE the border (between this element and others). Always transparent. Margins collapse vertically (larger wins); padding never collapses.

BWhat is Flexbox?+

One-dimensional layout system (row OR column). Container: display: flex. Key properties: justify-content (main axis alignment), align-items (cross axis), flex-wrap, gap. Items: flex-grow/shrink/basis, align-self, order. Use for: navbars, card rows, centering, equal-height columns.

BWhat is CSS Grid?+

Two-dimensional layout system (rows AND columns simultaneously). display: grid. Key: grid-template-columns/rows, gap, grid-template-areas. Items can span rows/columns. repeat(auto-fill, minmax(250px, 1fr)) for responsive layouts. Use for: page layouts, dashboards, card grids, any 2D arrangement.

BWhat is the difference between Flexbox and Grid?+

Flexbox: 1D (row OR column), content-driven sizing, items control their own size. Best for: components, navbars, alignment. Grid: 2D (rows AND columns), layout-driven, container controls item placement. Best for: page layouts, dashboards. Use both together — Grid for page structure, Flexbox for component internals.

BWhat is CSS specificity?+

How browsers decide which styles win when multiple rules target the same element. Specificity tuple: (inline, IDs, classes, elements). p = 0,0,0,1. .class = 0,0,1,0. #id = 0,1,0,0. An ID always beats any number of classes. !important overrides everything (avoid it).

BWhat are CSS selectors?+

Patterns to target elements. Element: p. Class: .btn. ID: #nav. Descendant: div p. Child: div > p. Adjacent sibling: h2 + p. General sibling: h2 ~ p. Attribute: [type='text']. Pseudo-class: :hover, :first-child, :nth-child(). Pseudo-element: ::before, ::after.

BWhat is the position property?+

static: default, normal flow. relative: offset from normal position, creates stacking context. absolute: removed from flow, positioned relative to nearest positioned ancestor. fixed: relative to viewport (stays on scroll). sticky: normal until scroll threshold, then fixed.

BWhat is z-index?+

Controls stacking order of positioned elements (not static). Higher z-index = on top. Only works on positioned elements (relative, absolute, fixed, sticky) and flex/grid children. Creates stacking contexts. Child z-index is relative to parent stacking context — a child can never escape its parent's context.

BWhat are pseudo-elements?+

::before and ::after insert virtual elements as first/last child. Require content property. ::placeholder styles input placeholders. ::selection styles selected text. ::first-line/::first-letter for typography. ::backdrop for dialog/fullscreen overlays. Double colon (::) is correct syntax.

BWhat is the display property?+

block: full width, new line. inline: no width/height, stays in flow. inline-block: inline but accepts dimensions. none: removed from layout (use visibility: hidden to hide but keep space). flex: flex container. grid: grid container. contents: element disappears, children move up.

IExplain CSS custom properties (variables).+

Defined with --name: value, used with var(--name, fallback). Cascade and inherit. Scope: :root for global, any selector for scoped. Can be changed dynamically with JS: element.style.setProperty('--color', 'red'). Enable theming, dynamic values, and reduce repetition. Unlike Sass variables, they're runtime values.

IWhat are container queries?+

Style elements based on their CONTAINER's size, not the viewport. container-type: inline-size on parent, then @container (min-width: 400px) { } for children. Revolutionary for reusable components — a card adapts whether it's in a sidebar or main content. Container query units: cqi, cqw, cqh.

IWhat is the :has() selector?+

The 'parent selector' — styles an element based on its children/siblings. .card:has(img) targets cards containing images. form:has(:invalid) .submit { opacity: 0.5 } dims submit if form has errors. h2:has(+ p) targets h2 followed by p. The most powerful CSS selector ever added — many things that required JS are now CSS-only.

IExplain CSS nesting.+

Native CSS now supports nesting (like Sass): .card { color: black; &:hover { color: blue; } & .title { font-size: 1.5rem; } @media (width >= 768px) { padding: 2rem; } }. The & represents the parent selector. Reduces repetition, improves readability. Supported in all modern browsers since 2023.

IWhat is the @layer at-rule?+

Controls cascade order explicitly: @layer base, components, utilities;. Later layers win over earlier ones, regardless of specificity within layers. Un-layered styles beat ALL layers. Use for: managing CSS framework overrides, organizing large codebases, ensuring utility classes always win (Tailwind uses this).

IWhat is BEM methodology?+

Block Element Modifier: .block__element--modifier. Block: standalone component (.card). Element: part of block (.card__title). Modifier: variation (.card--featured). Benefits: predictable naming, flat specificity, clear relationships. Drawback: verbose class names. Still widely used in non-utility-first codebases.

IWhat is margin collapsing?+

Adjacent vertical margins collapse — only the larger one applies. margin-bottom: 20px + margin-top: 30px = 30px gap (not 50px). Happens: between siblings, parent-child (when no padding/border separates them), empty elements. Does NOT happen: with flex/grid items, floated elements, absolutely positioned elements, or elements with overflow != visible.

IWhat is the difference between em, rem, px, %, vh/vw?+

px: absolute (1 CSS pixel). em: relative to parent font-size (compounds!). rem: relative to root font-size (predictable). %: relative to parent's property. vh/vw: viewport height/width. dvh/dvw: dynamic viewport (accounts for mobile URL bar). cqi: container query inline size. Use rem for font sizes, px for borders, % or fr for layout.

IHow do you center an element vertically and horizontally?+

Flex: display: flex; justify-content: center; align-items: center;. Grid: display: grid; place-items: center; (shortest). Position: position: absolute; inset: 0; margin: auto; (needs defined width/height). Transform: position: absolute; top: 50%; left: 50%; transform: translate(-50%, -50%);.

IWhat is content-visibility?+

content-visibility: auto skips rendering of offscreen elements. The browser only computes layout/paint for visible elements. contain-intrinsic-size: auto 500px provides estimated size to prevent scroll jumps. Can improve initial render time by 50%+ on long pages. Think of it as 'virtualization for CSS.'

AExplain stacking contexts in depth.+

A stacking context is a 3D conceptualization where elements have z-axis position. Created by: positioned elements with z-index, opacity < 1, transform/filter/clip-path, flex/grid children with z-index, isolation: isolate. Key rule: z-index is RELATIVE to the stacking context — a child cannot appear above a sibling of its parent's stacking context, regardless of z-index value. This is why z-index 'doesn't work' sometimes.

AExplain the CSS contain property.+

contain: layout | paint | size | style. Tells browser that this element is isolated — changes inside don't affect outside. layout: layout of children doesn't affect ancestors. paint: content doesn't paint outside bounds. size: element's size isn't affected by children. content = layout paint style. strict = all. Enables browser to skip work during recalculation. Foundation of content-visibility.

AWhat is the CSS cascade in detail?+

Order of priority (highest to lowest): 1) User agent !important. 2) User !important. 3) Author !important. 4) Author styles (normal). 5) User styles (normal). 6) User agent styles. Within same origin: a) @layer order (un-layered > layered). b) Specificity. c) Source order (last wins). The cascade is more nuanced than most developers realize — @layer fundamentally changed it.

AHow do CSS animations perform vs JavaScript animations?+

CSS transform and opacity animations run on the GPU compositor thread — they don't trigger layout/paint and don't block the main thread. CSS width/height/top animations trigger layout (expensive). JS animations via requestAnimationFrame run on main thread — can be janky if main thread is busy. Web Animations API (element.animate()) can be compositor-promoted like CSS. For best performance: use CSS transforms, avoid layout-triggering properties.

AWhat is subgrid?+

grid-template-columns: subgrid on a grid child makes it inherit parent's column/row tracks. Solves: aligning nested grids with parent (card content aligning across cards). Without subgrid, each card has independent internal grid — headers/footers may not align. With subgrid, they share the same grid lines. Supported in all modern browsers since 2023.

AWhat are scroll-driven animations?+

CSS animations linked to scroll position instead of time. animation-timeline: scroll() for scroll progress, animation-timeline: view() for element visibility. animation-range: entry 0% cover 40% controls when animation plays. No JavaScript needed. Use for: reveal-on-scroll, parallax, progress indicators. Spec includes ScrollTimeline and ViewTimeline JS APIs for complex cases.

AExplain the will-change property.+

will-change: transform hints the browser to optimize for upcoming changes — creates a new compositor layer, allocates GPU memory. Improves animation performance BUT: uses extra memory, creates stacking context, should be added just before animation and removed after. Don't apply globally (* { will-change: transform }) — it wastes resources. Use sparingly, remove when done.

AHow would you architect CSS for a large application?+

Design tokens: CSS custom properties for colors, spacing, typography, shadows. Reset/base: Modern reset (Tailwind preflight or custom). Layout: CSS Grid for page, Flexbox for components. Components: CSS Modules or Tailwind for scoping. Utilities: Tailwind or custom utility classes in highest @layer. Theming: CSS variables + prefers-color-scheme + data-theme attribute. Performance: Critical CSS inlined, rest lazy-loaded, content-visibility for long pages. Organization: @layer for cascade control, logical grouping.

BWhat is the cascade in CSS?+

The algorithm that determines which styles apply when multiple rules target the same element. Order: 1) Importance (!important). 2) Origin (author > user > browser). 3) Layer order (@layer). 4) Specificity. 5) Source order (last wins). Understanding the cascade is the foundation of CSS mastery.

BWhat is a CSS reset/normalize?+

Reset (Eric Meyer's): strips ALL default browser styles to zero. Normalize (normalize.css): makes defaults consistent across browsers while preserving useful ones. Modern approach: Tailwind Preflight or custom reset with box-sizing: border-box, margin reset, and sensible defaults.

BWhat is the difference between visibility: hidden and display: none?+

display: none: element is completely removed from layout (no space, no interaction, not accessible). visibility: hidden: element is invisible but still takes up space. Screen readers skip both. For accessible hiding (visible to screen readers only), use the sr-only/visually-hidden class pattern.

BWhat is the float property?+

Originally for text wrapping around images. float: left|right removes element from normal flow and pushes it to one side. Parent collapses unless cleared (clear: both) or uses overflow: hidden. Don't use for layout — Flexbox and Grid have completely replaced float-based layouts.

BWhat are pseudo-classes?+

Select elements based on state: :hover (mouse over), :focus (focused), :active (being clicked), :visited (visited link), :first-child, :last-child, :nth-child(2n) (even children), :not(.class), :focus-visible (keyboard focus only), :disabled, :checked, :empty (no children).

BWhat is the overflow property?+

visible: content overflows (default). hidden: clips overflow, creates BFC. scroll: always shows scrollbar. auto: scrollbar only when needed. clip: like hidden but doesn't create scroll container. Can set per axis: overflow-x: hidden; overflow-y: auto;.

IWhat is a Block Formatting Context (BFC)?+

A layout context where floats are contained and margins don't collapse through it. Created by: overflow (not visible), display: flow-root (modern, no side effects), float, position: absolute/fixed, flex/grid items. display: flow-root is the intended way to create a BFC.

IWhat are logical properties?+

Direction-aware alternatives to physical properties: margin-inline-start (left in LTR, right in RTL), padding-block (top + bottom), inset-inline (left + right), border-block-end (bottom). Enable RTL support without separate stylesheets. inline = horizontal, block = vertical (in horizontal writing modes).

IWhat is the clamp() function?+

clamp(min, preferred, max) — value that scales with preferred but never goes below min or above max. Most common use: font-size: clamp(1rem, 2.5vw, 2rem) — fluid typography that adapts to viewport. Also works for spacing, widths, any length value. Eliminates many media queries.

IWhat is the aspect-ratio property?+

aspect-ratio: 16 / 9 maintains a ratio regardless of content/container size. Replaces the old padding-bottom hack. Use for: video containers, image placeholders, card layouts. Combined with object-fit: cover for images/video that fill the ratio without distortion.

IWhat is object-fit?+

Controls how replaced content (img, video) fills its container. cover: fills container, crops if needed (most common). contain: fits inside container, may have gaps. fill: stretches to fill (distorts). none: original size. scale-down: smallest of none or contain. object-position controls alignment.

IWhat is the difference between transform and position for moving elements?+

transform: translate() moves the element visually without affecting layout — other elements don't reflow. It's GPU-composited (fast). position: relative/absolute with top/left also moves elements but may trigger layout recalculation. For animations, ALWAYS use transform — it's orders of magnitude faster.

IHow do CSS transitions work?+

transition: property duration timing-function delay. Animates between two states (e.g., normal → hover). Only works for properties that have calculable intermediate values (not display). transition: all is convenient but less performant — specify exact properties. Timing functions: ease, linear, ease-in-out, or cubic-bezier().

IWhat is the gap property?+

Sets spacing between flex/grid items without margin on individual items. gap: 1rem (both axes) or row-gap: 1rem; column-gap: 2rem;. Works in Flexbox (since 2020) and Grid. Advantages over margin: no double margins, no negative margins for edge items, simpler responsive adjustments.

AExplain CSS Paint API (Houdini).+

Part of CSS Houdini — allows custom paint worklets: CSS.paintWorklet.addModule('painter.js'). Inside the worklet, you draw using Canvas-like API. Used as: background: paint(my-painter). Enables: custom backgrounds, borders, decorations without images. Still limited browser support but shows the future of CSS extensibility.

AWhat is the @scope at-rule?+

@scope (.card) to (.card-footer) { p { color: gray; } } — styles only apply between the scoping root (.card) and the limit (.card-footer). Without 'to', scopes to all descendants. Provides true CSS scoping without Shadow DOM or CSS Modules. Allows donut scoping — style content between two boundaries. Chrome supports since 2023.

AHow does CSS Grid auto-placement algorithm work?+

When items don't have explicit grid-column/row, the auto-placement algorithm places them. grid-auto-flow: row (default): fills row by row. column: fills column by column. dense: backfills gaps with smaller items. grid-auto-rows/grid-auto-columns set implicit track sizes. Understanding auto-placement is key for masonry-like layouts.

AWhat are anchor-positioned elements?+

CSS Anchor Positioning (new spec): position elements relative to another element without JS. anchor-name: --my-anchor on target, position-anchor: --my-anchor on positioned element. top: anchor(bottom) positions below the anchor. Enables: tooltips, popovers, floating menus that follow their trigger. Replaces Floating UI/Popper.js for many cases.

BWhat is the inherit, initial, unset, revert values?+

inherit: use parent's value. initial: use CSS specification default. unset: inherit if property normally inherits, else initial. revert: use browser's default stylesheet value (not CSS spec). revert-layer: revert to previous @layer. Use revert to reset to browser defaults, unset for a cleaner approach.

BWhat is the min-height vs height difference?+

height: 500px is FIXED — content overflows if it's taller. min-height: 500px sets a minimum but GROWS with content. Use min-height for most layouts (especially min-height: 100dvh for full-page sections). Use fixed height only for explicitly sized elements (images, avatars).

BWhat is the line-height property?+

Sets the height of a line box. Unitless value recommended: line-height: 1.6 (1.6× the font size). With units: line-height: 24px (fixed, doesn't scale). Unitless values are inherited as a multiplier; unit values are inherited as the computed value. This is why line-height: 1.6 is better than line-height: 24px.

IWhat is the outline vs border difference?+

border: part of the box model (affects layout, adds to element size with content-box). outline: drawn OUTSIDE the border, does NOT affect layout, can't have radius (until recently), can be offset with outline-offset. Focus outlines: never remove them (outline: none) without a visible replacement. outline-offset: 2px looks better than touching the element.

IWhat is the pointer-events property?+

pointer-events: none makes element invisible to mouse/touch (clicks pass through). pointer-events: auto restores. Use for: disabled overlays, decorative elements that shouldn't block clicks, custom cursors/tooltips that shouldn't interfere. Works on SVG too with additional values (fill, stroke, etc.).

IWhat is the isolation property?+

isolation: isolate creates a new stacking context without side effects (unlike opacity, transform, or z-index which also change rendering). Use when you want to contain z-index without affecting the element's appearance. Common: isolate a component so its internal z-indexes don't leak to siblings.

IWhat is the place-items / place-content / place-self shorthand?+

place-items: center = align-items: center; justify-items: center. place-content: center = align-content: center; justify-content: center. place-self: center = align-self: center; justify-self: center. display: grid; place-items: center; is the shortest centering in CSS — 2 properties to center anything.

IWhat is the writing-mode property?+

writing-mode: vertical-rl makes text flow top-to-bottom, right-to-left (Chinese, Japanese). vertical-lr: left-to-right columns. horizontal-tb: normal (default). Changes how block/inline axes work. Logical properties (margin-block, padding-inline) automatically adapt. Used for: vertical text, sideways headings, CJK layouts.

AExplain the paint, layout, and composite rendering stages.+

Layout (Reflow): Calculates geometry of ALL affected elements — triggered by: width/height/margin/padding changes, DOM modifications, font loading. EXPENSIVE. Paint: Fills pixels — triggered by: color, background, shadow, visibility changes. Moderate cost. Composite: Combines layers on GPU — triggered by: transform, opacity ONLY. CHEAP. Goal: animate ONLY transform/opacity to stay in composite-only. Use will-change to promote elements to their own layer.

AWhat is CSS Containment?+

contain: layout: element's internal layout doesn't affect external. contain: paint: content doesn't render outside bounds. contain: size: element's size doesn't depend on children. contain: style: counters/quotes scoped. contain: content = layout + paint + style. contain: strict = all. Foundation of content-visibility. Tells browser it can skip work when recalculating — significant perf win for complex pages.

AWhat is CSS Grid masonry layout?+

Experimental: grid-template-rows: masonry creates Pinterest-style stacking where items fill vertical gaps. Currently only in Firefox (behind flag). Polyfills exist. Alternative: multi-column layout (column-count) — simpler but items flow top-to-bottom per column, not left-to-right. JavaScript libraries (Masonry.js) are still the reliable cross-browser solution in 2026.

AHow does the new CSS @starting-style rule work?+

@starting-style defines initial styles for elements entering the DOM, enabling transitions for elements that start with display: none.
