Previously impossible — transitions need a starting state. @starting-style { dialog[open] { opacity: 0; } } dialog[open] { opacity: 1; transition: opacity 0.3s; }. Enables CSS-only modal/tooltip entry animations without JavaScript.

AWhat is the interpolate-size property?+

interpolate-size: allow-keywords on :root enables transitioning between keyword sizes (height: 0 → height: auto) — previously impossible without JavaScript. This means CSS-only accordions with smooth height animations: .content { height: 0; overflow: hidden; transition: height 0.3s; } .open .content { height: auto; }. Game-changing for collapsible content.

Frontend system design is the #1 topic that separates senior engineers from mid-level. These questions test your ability to architect entire applications.

How to Approach Any Frontend System Design

The Framework (use this for every question)

1. Requirements (5 min): Clarify functional requirements (what does it DO?), non-functional requirements (performance, scale, accessibility), constraints (browser support, offline, i18n).

2. High-Level Architecture (10 min): Component hierarchy, data flow, state management strategy, API design, routing structure.

3. Deep Dive (20 min): Pick 2-3 areas to go deep on based on the interviewer's interest. Performance optimizations, caching strategy, error handling, accessibility, real-time updates.

4. Trade-offs (5 min): Discuss alternatives you considered and why you chose your approach. Show you understand the trade-offs.

System design interviews are NOT about the 'right answer.' They're about your thought process: how you break down problems, consider trade-offs, and communicate decisions. Talk out loud, ask questions, draw diagrams.

Component Architecture Patterns

Atomic Design

Atoms: Button, Input, Label — smallest units. Molecules: SearchBar (Input + Button), FormField (Label + Input + Error). Organisms: Header (Logo + Nav + SearchBar), ProductCard. Templates: Page layouts with placeholder content. Pages: Templates with real data.

Feature-Based Architecture

src/
├── features/
│   ├── auth/           # Everything for auth
│   │   ├── components/ # LoginForm, SignupForm
│   │   ├── hooks/      # useAuth, useSession
│   │   ├── api/        # auth API functions
│   │   └── types.ts
│   ├── products/
│   └── cart/
├── shared/             # Cross-feature reusables
│   ├── ui/             # Button, Modal, Input
│   ├── hooks/          # useDebounce, useLocalStorage
│   └── utils/          # formatDate, cn()
└── app/                # Routes/pages

Design System Design

When asked to design a design system (Meta, Airbnb, Google-style question):

Core Layers

1. Design Tokens: Colors, spacing, typography, shadows, borders as CSS variables or JS constants. Single source of truth. Support theming (light/dark, brand variants).

2. Primitive Components: Button, Input, Select, Checkbox, Radio, Toggle, Badge, Avatar, Tooltip. Zero business logic. Maximum accessibility. Composable via props (variant, size, disabled).

3. Composite Components: Dialog, Dropdown, Tabs, Accordion, Toast, Pagination — built from primitives. Manage their own state (open/close, active tab).

4. Pattern Library: Authentication forms, data tables, navigation patterns — built from composites. Include documentation and usage examples.

Technical Decisions

Styling: CSS variables for tokens + CSS Modules or Tailwind for components. Distribution: Monorepo with packages per component, or single package with tree-shaking. Documentation: Storybook for component playground. Versioning: Semantic versioning, changelogs, migration guides. Testing: Visual regression (Chromatic), unit tests, accessibility audit.

State Management Architecture

How to choose and structure state management at scale:

State Categories

Server State: Data from APIs. Use TanStack Query — handles caching, deduplication, background refresh, optimistic updates, pagination, infinite scroll. This is 60-80% of most app state.

Client State: UI state (modals, sidebars, theme). Use Zustand — tiny, simple, no boilerplate. Or React context for infrequent changes (auth, locale).

URL State: Search params, filters, pagination. Use the URL as state — useSearchParams(). Shareable, bookmarkable, back-button works.

Form State: Input values, validation, dirty/touched. Use React Hook Form or Server Actions. Don't put form state in global store.

The biggest mistake in frontend architecture is putting everything in a global store (Redux). Server data belongs in a cache (TanStack Query), URL state belongs in the URL, form state belongs in the form. Only truly global UI state (theme, auth, sidebar) needs a global store.

Performance Architecture

Core Web Vitals Strategy

LCP (Largest Contentful Paint) < 2.5s: Preload hero image, use fetchpriority='high', SSR/SSG for fast HTML, optimize fonts (preload, swap), CDN for static assets.

INP (Interaction to Next Paint) < 200ms: Minimize main-thread work, defer non-critical JS, use startTransition for expensive updates, break long tasks with scheduler.yield().

CLS (Cumulative Layout Shift) < 0.1: Always set width/height on images/video, use min-height on dynamic sections, font-display: swap with fallback metrics, avoid inserting content above viewport.

Bundle Optimization

Code Splitting: Route-based (automatic in Next.js), component-based (React.lazy), third-party (dynamic imports). Tree Shaking: Use ESM, avoid barrel files that import everything, check bundle with source-map-explorer. Compression: Brotli > gzip. Lazy Loading: Images below fold, heavy components, third-party scripts.

Real-Time Architecture

Approaches (ranked by complexity)

1. Polling: setInterval + fetch. Simple but wasteful. Good for: dashboards refreshing every 30s. Use TanStack Query's refetchInterval.

2. Long Polling: Request stays open until server has data. Less wasteful, still HTTP. Good for: chat apps with moderate traffic.

3. Server-Sent Events (SSE): One-way stream from server. Simple API, auto-reconnect, works with HTTP/2. Good for: notifications, live feeds, stock tickers.

4. WebSockets: Full-duplex, bidirectional. Most complex but most powerful. Good for: real-time collaboration, gaming, live chat. Libraries: Socket.io, Ably, Pusher.

5. WebRTC: Peer-to-peer. For: video calls, screen sharing, file transfer between browsers.

Start simple. Most 'real-time' features work fine with polling every 5-30 seconds via TanStack Query. Only reach for WebSockets when you truly need sub-second bidirectional communication (collaborative editing, gaming).

Micro-Frontends Architecture

Approaches

1. Module Federation (Webpack/Vite): Share dependencies at runtime between separately deployed apps. Each team deploys independently. Most popular approach.

2. iframe-based: Complete isolation but poor UX (no shared routing, styling challenges). Simplest to implement.

3. Web Components: Framework-agnostic custom elements. Each team uses their preferred framework internally.

4. Route-based composition: Different apps own different routes. Reverse proxy routes to correct app. Simplest at infrastructure level.

5. Build-time integration: NPM packages published by each team, composed in a shell app. Strong typing but requires coordinated deployments.

Shared Concerns

Authentication: Shared JWT/session via cookies. Styling: Shared design tokens (CSS variables). Communication: Custom events, shared state library, URL params. Performance: Shared vendor chunks, deduplication.

Security in Frontend Architecture

XSS (Cross-Site Scripting)

Inject malicious scripts into your page. Prevention: Never use innerHTML with user input, React auto-escapes JSX (but not dangerouslySetInnerHTML), Content Security Policy (CSP) headers, sanitize rich text with DOMPurify.

CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery)

Trick user's browser into making unwanted requests. Prevention: SameSite cookies (Lax or Strict), CSRF tokens, verify Origin/Referer headers.

Other Concerns

Clickjacking: X-Frame-Options: DENY header. Dependency attacks: npm audit, lockfiles, Dependabot. Secrets: NEVER expose API keys in client code (use server-side proxy). Auth: httpOnly + Secure + SameSite cookies > localStorage for tokens.

NEVER store auth tokens in localStorage — any XSS vulnerability can steal them. Use httpOnly cookies — JavaScript can't access them, so XSS can't steal them.
BWhat is the cursor property?+

Controls mouse cursor appearance: pointer (clickable), default (arrow), text (I-beam), wait (loading), not-allowed (disabled), grab/grabbing, crosshair, move, resize variants, zoom-in/out, none (hidden). Use appropriate cursors to communicate interactivity.

BWhat are CSS shorthand properties?+

margin: 10px 20px (TB LR). border: 1px solid red. background: url() center/cover no-repeat. font: italic bold 16px/1.5 Arial. flex: 1 0 200px (grow shrink basis). inset: 0 (all sides). place-items: center. Gotcha: shorthands reset omitted sub-properties to initial values.

BHow do you truncate text with ellipsis?+

Single line: overflow: hidden; white-space: nowrap; text-overflow: ellipsis;. Multi-line: display: -webkit-box; -webkit-line-clamp: 3; -webkit-box-orient: vertical; overflow: hidden;. The multi-line approach works in all modern browsers despite the -webkit prefix.

BWhat is the box-shadow property?+

box-shadow: x-offset y-offset blur spread color. Example: 0 4px 12px rgba(0,0,0,0.1). Multiple shadows: comma-separated. inset for inner shadow. filter: drop-shadow() follows element shape (including transparent areas) — better for irregular shapes and PNG/SVG.

BWhat is opacity vs rgba for transparency?+

opacity: 0.5 affects the ENTIRE element INCLUDING children. rgba(0,0,0,0.5) or color-mix(in srgb, black 50%, transparent) only affects that specific property. Use color functions for transparent backgrounds; use opacity only for fading entire elements.

IWhat is the appearance property?+

appearance: none removes ALL native form styling (checkboxes, selects, radios). Lets you build completely custom controls. Warning: you lose native keyboard handling, focus rings, and screen reader behavior — must re-implement for accessibility. Test thoroughly.

IWhat is backdrop-filter?+

backdrop-filter: blur(16px) saturate(180%) applies effects to the area BEHIND the element, creating frosted-glass effects. Element needs semi-transparent background to see through. GPU-composited but can be heavy on mobile devices.

IWhat is text-wrap: balance and pretty?+

text-wrap: balance: distributes text evenly across lines — no more one lonely word on the last line of a heading. text-wrap: pretty: prevents orphaned words at paragraph ends. Both are new (2023-2024) and massively improve typography with zero effort. Apply balance to headings, pretty to body text.

IWhat is accent-color?+

accent-color: #6366f1 — one line themes ALL native form controls: checkboxes, radio buttons, range sliders, progress bars. Automatic contrast text color for checked states. The easiest design polish in CSS. Apply to :root for site-wide theming.

AWhat are modern CSS color spaces (oklch, display-p3)?+

oklch(70% 0.15 250): perceptually uniform — equal lightness steps look equal. Better for generating color scales. color(display-p3 1 0 0): wider gamut colors on supported screens. color-mix(in oklch, red 30%, blue): blends in perceptually accurate space. oklch is the future of CSS color — produces better gradients and predictable lightness.

IWhat is scroll-behavior: smooth?+

Apply to html for smooth scrolling on anchor links and scrollTo(). No JavaScript needed. Programmatic: element.scrollIntoView({ behavior: "smooth" }). Always respect preferences: @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) { html { scroll-behavior: auto; } }.

IWhat is the multi-column layout?+

column-count: 3 or column-width: 250px creates newspaper-style text columns. column-gap and column-rule for spacing/dividers. break-inside: avoid prevents elements splitting across columns. Use for text-heavy content, not page layout.

Common system design interview questions with solution frameworks.

Design: Infinite Scroll Feed (Instagram/Twitter)

Key Challenges

Performance with thousands of items, smooth scrolling, memory management, loading states, error recovery.

Solution Architecture

Virtualization: Only render items visible in viewport (~20 items). Use @tanstack/react-virtual. Items outside viewport are removed from DOM.

Data Fetching: TanStack Query's useInfiniteQuery with cursor-based pagination. Intersection Observer on a sentinel element triggers next page fetch.

Optimistic Updates: Like/unlike with useOptimistic. Show change instantly, revert on error.

Image Loading: Lazy load with loading='lazy', blur placeholder, progressive JPEG/AVIF. Skeleton screens during load.

State: Each post is a cache entry in TanStack Query. Mutations invalidate specific queries. New posts prepended without refetching entire feed.

Design: Real-Time Collaborative Editor (Google Docs)

Key Challenges

Concurrent editing without conflicts, real-time sync, offline support, cursor presence.

Solution Architecture

Conflict Resolution: CRDT (Conflict-free Replicated Data Type) libraries: Yjs, Automerge. Each client has a local replica that syncs automatically. CRDTs guarantee convergence without central authority.

Transport: WebSocket for real-time sync. Fallback to polling for unreliable connections. Use Y-websocket or Liveblocks.

Editor: ProseMirror, TipTap, or Lexical with CRDT integration. Rich text model maps to CRDT operations.

Presence: Broadcast cursor positions and selections via WebSocket. Debounce position updates (50-100ms).

Offline: CRDT operations stored in IndexedDB. Sync when reconnected. Works because CRDTs handle merge automatically.

Design: E-Commerce Product Page (Amazon)

Key Challenges

Performance with SEO, dynamic pricing/stock, image gallery, reviews, recommendations, cart interaction.

Solution Architecture

Rendering: Partial Prerendering (PPR). Static: layout, product info, image gallery. Dynamic: price, stock, personalized recommendations, cart count. Static parts from CDN in <50ms.

Images: next/image with AVIF, blur placeholder, zoom on hover (separate high-res lazy-loaded). Gallery with virtual scroll for many images.

Cart: Zustand store + optimistic updates. Add-to-cart shows instant feedback. Server confirms async.

Reviews: Paginated, loaded below-fold with Suspense. Sort/filter as URL params (shareable).

SEO: Structured data (Product schema), canonical URLs, generateMetadata for dynamic titles, breadcrumbs.

Design: Chat Application (Slack/Discord)

Key Challenges

Real-time messaging, channels/DMs, message history, typing indicators, file uploads, notifications.

Solution Architecture

Transport: WebSocket (Socket.io or native WS) for real-time messages, typing indicators, presence. REST/Server Actions for: message history, file uploads, channel management.

Message List: Virtualized list (bottom-anchored). Load older messages on scroll up (reverse pagination). Optimistic send: show message immediately with 'pending' state.

State: TanStack Query for message history (cached per channel). Zustand for UI state (active channel, sidebar, compose). WebSocket events update TanStack Query cache directly.

Notifications: Service Worker + Push API for background notifications. Badge count via shared state. Permission request UX.

File Uploads: Presigned URLs to S3/R2. Client uploads directly (no server proxy). Progress bar with XMLHttpRequest or fetch + ReadableStream.

42 interview questions from very basic to very advanced. Click to reveal answers.

Showing 42 questions
BWhat is a SPA vs MPA?+

SPA (Single Page Application): One HTML file, JS handles all navigation (client-side routing). Fast transitions but: slow initial load, SEO challenges, complex state. MPA (Multi Page Application): Each page is a new HTML file from server. Simpler, better SEO, but full page reloads. Modern: Next.js/Nuxt combine both — SPA-like transitions with server rendering.

BWhat is client-side rendering (CSR) vs server-side rendering (SSR)?+

CSR: Browser downloads empty HTML + JS bundle, JS renders everything. Pros: fast subsequent navigation. Cons: slow first load, no SEO for dynamic content. SSR: Server renders HTML for each request. Pros: fast first paint, SEO friendly. Cons: server load per request. Modern: use SSR for initial load, CSR for navigation (hybrid).

BWhat is a CDN?+

Content Delivery Network — globally distributed servers that cache and serve static assets (HTML, CSS, JS, images) from the nearest location to the user. Reduces latency from 200-500ms to 20-50ms. Use for: static files, pre-rendered pages (SSG/ISR). Providers: Cloudflare, AWS CloudFront, Fastly, Vercel Edge Network.

BWhat is lazy loading?+

Deferring loading of non-critical resources until they're needed. Images: loading='lazy'. Components: React.lazy() + Suspense. Routes: dynamic import(). Benefits: faster initial load, less bandwidth. Implement with: native lazy loading, Intersection Observer, route-based code splitting.

BWhat is responsive design?+

Designing for all screen sizes. Techniques: 1) Fluid layouts (%, fr, flex). 2) Media queries (@media). 3) Responsive images (srcset). 4) Mobile-first approach. 5) Modern: container queries, clamp(), auto-fit/auto-fill grid. 6) Viewport units (vh/vw/dvh). The goal: one codebase, every device.

IWhat are Core Web Vitals?+

Google's metrics for user experience: LCP (Largest Contentful Paint) < 2.5s: How fast the main content loads. INP (Interaction to Next Paint) < 200ms: How fast the page responds to user input. CLS (Cumulative Layout Shift) < 0.1: How much the layout shifts during load. They directly affect SEO ranking. Measured by Chrome UX Report, Lighthouse, PageSpeed Insights.

IHow would you optimize a website's performance?+

Network: CDN, compression (Brotli), HTTP/2, preload/preconnect critical resources. Rendering: SSR/SSG, critical CSS, above-fold optimization, defer non-critical JS. Assets: Image optimization (AVIF/WebP), responsive images, lazy loading, font optimization (swap + preload). Runtime: Code splitting, tree shaking, virtualization for long lists, debounce/throttle, web workers for heavy computation.

IWhat is code splitting?+

Breaking your JS bundle into smaller chunks loaded on demand. Route-based: Each page is a separate chunk (automatic in Next.js). Component-based: React.lazy(() => import('./Heavy')). Library-based: Dynamic import for heavy libraries (const pdf = await import('pdfjs')). Result: smaller initial bundle = faster load. Analyze with webpack-bundle-analyzer or source-map-explorer.

IExplain caching strategies.+

Cache-First: Check cache before network. Best for: static assets (CSS, JS, images). Network-First: Try network, fallback to cache. Best for: API responses, dynamic content. Stale-While-Revalidate: Serve cached immediately, update cache in background. Best for: content that changes but freshness isn't critical. Cache-Only: Offline-first apps. Network-Only: Always fresh data. Headers: Cache-Control, ETag, Last-Modified.

IWhat is tree shaking?+

Dead code elimination — removing unused exports from the final bundle. Requires: ES modules (import/export), static analysis at build time. Webpack/Rollup/Turbopack analyze which exports are actually imported and remove the rest. Gotchas: barrel files (index.ts re-exporting everything) can prevent tree shaking — import directly from source files instead.

IHow do you handle authentication in a frontend application?+

Token storage: httpOnly cookies (secure, immune to XSS). Never localStorage for tokens. Flow: Login → server sets httpOnly cookie → cookie sent automatically with every request. JWT: Access token (short-lived, 15min) + refresh token (httpOnly cookie, 7 days). OAuth/OIDC: For third-party login (Google, GitHub). Libraries: Auth.js, Clerk, auth0. Server Components: Read session server-side, never expose tokens to client.

IWhat is the difference between HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, and HTTP/3?+

HTTP/1.1: One request per connection, head-of-line blocking, text protocol. HTTP/2: Multiplexing (many requests over one connection), header compression, server push, binary protocol. Domain sharding unnecessary. HTTP/3: QUIC transport (UDP instead of TCP), faster connection setup, no TCP head-of-line blocking. Automatic in most CDNs.

IHow do you handle error boundaries in a frontend app?+

Component level: React error boundaries catch rendering errors, show fallback UI, log to error service. Route level: error.tsx in Next.js catches per-route. Global: global-error.tsx for root errors. API errors: Try/catch in Server Actions, error states in useActionState, TanStack Query error states. Monitoring: Sentry, Datadog, LogRocket for production error tracking.

IWhat is a Service Worker?+

A JavaScript file that runs as a network proxy between your app and the internet. Can: intercept fetch requests, serve cached responses (offline support), receive push notifications, run background sync. Lifecycle: install → activate → fetch. Registered with navigator.serviceWorker.register(). Foundation of Progressive Web Apps (PWAs). Must be served over HTTPS.

IWhat is the PRPL pattern?+

Push critical resources for initial route (preload). Render initial route ASAP. Pre-cache remaining routes (service worker). Lazy-load remaining routes on demand. Optimizes for first load AND subsequent navigations. Implemented naturally by frameworks like Next.js with SSR + code splitting + prefetching.

ADesign a frontend monitoring and observability system.+

Error tracking: Global error handler + error boundaries → Sentry/Datadog. Capture: stack traces, user context, breadcrumbs (actions before error). Performance: Web Vitals (LCP, INP, CLS) via web-vitals library → analytics pipeline. User behavior: Session replay (LogRocket, FullStory) for debugging. Custom metrics: Business KPIs (conversion, engagement) via custom events. Alerting: Error rate spikes, CWV regression, API latency thresholds. Dashboard: Grafana/Datadog for real-time visibility.

AHow would you design the frontend for a design system?+

Tokens: CSS variables for colors, spacing, typography, shadows. Components: Primitive (Button, Input) → Composite (Dialog, Dropdown). Each component: accessible by default (ARIA), customizable (variants, sizes via props), documented (Storybook), tested (visual regression + unit). Distribution: Monorepo with per-component packages, tree-shakeable. Theming: CSS variables + data-theme attribute. Versioning: Semver, auto-generated changelogs, codemods for migrations.

AHow would you architect a micro-frontend application?+

Composition: Module Federation (runtime dependency sharing) or route-based (different apps for different paths). Shared concerns: Design tokens (CSS variables), auth (shared cookies), routing (shell app controls top-level). Communication: Custom events, URL params, shared state library. Deployment: Independent CI/CD per team. Performance: Shared vendor chunks, deduplication, lazy loading boundaries. Trade-offs: Increased complexity, harder debugging, bundle size coordination. Only use when team autonomy is critical.

AHow do you handle internationalization (i18n) at scale?+

Architecture: Locale in URL (/en/page), middleware for detection (Accept-Language + geo + cookie). Translations: JSON files per locale, loaded server-side (Server Components). RTL support: CSS logical properties (margin-inline, padding-block). Formatting: Intl API for dates, numbers, currencies. Content: Translation management system (Crowdin, Phrase) for non-devs. Performance: Only load current locale's strings, lazy-load others. Testing: Pseudo-localization to catch layout issues.

ADesign a real-time collaborative whiteboard.+

Canvas: HTML Canvas API or WebGL (better for complex graphics). Sync: CRDT (Yjs) for conflict-free collaborative state. Each stroke/shape is a CRDT operation. Transport: WebSocket for low-latency sync. Performance: Spatial partitioning — only sync/render visible viewport area. Virtualize off-screen content. Offline: IndexedDB for local state, CRDT handles merge on reconnect. History: Undo/redo stack per user via CRDT operations. Presence: Cursor positions + selected elements broadcast at 10-20fps.

AHow do you handle state management at scale?+

Classification: Server state (TanStack Query — 70% of most apps), URL state (search params — 10%), client state (Zustand — 15%), form state (React Hook Form — 5%). Don't put everything in one global store. Architecture: Feature-based stores, selectors for derived state, middleware for side effects. Normalization: For relational data, normalize in cache (TanStack Query handles this). Optimistic updates: Show instant feedback, reconcile with server response.

ADesign an accessible data table with sorting, filtering, and pagination.+

Semantics: <table>, <thead>, <tbody>, <th scope='col'>. Sort: aria-sort='ascending|descending|none' on <th>, button inside th for click target. Filter: Controlled inputs above table, apply filters as URL params (shareable, bookmarkable). Pagination: role='navigation', aria-label='Pagination', announce page changes with aria-live. Performance: Virtual rows for 1000+ items (@tanstack/react-table + react-virtual). Responsiveness: Horizontal scroll wrapper or card layout on mobile.

BWhat is a REST API?+

Representational State Transfer — an architectural style for APIs. Principles: stateless requests, resource-based URLs, HTTP methods for actions (GET=read, POST=create, PUT/PATCH=update, DELETE=remove), standard status codes (200, 201, 400, 401, 404, 500). Endpoints: /api/users (collection), /api/users/123 (single resource).

BWhat is the difference between GET and POST?+

GET: Read data, parameters in URL, cacheable, bookmarkable, idempotent (same result every time), limited URL length. POST: Create/modify data, body contains data, not cached, not bookmarkable, not idempotent, no size limit. Use GET for fetching, POST for mutations.

BWhat is CORS?+

Cross-Origin Resource Sharing — browser security mechanism. Requests to a different origin (protocol + domain + port) are blocked unless the server sends Access-Control-Allow-Origin headers. Preflight requests (OPTIONS) check permission for non-simple requests. Server must explicitly allow cross-origin access.

BWhat is progressive enhancement vs graceful degradation?+

Progressive enhancement: Start with basic functionality (HTML), add layers (CSS, JS). Works for everyone, enhanced for modern browsers. Graceful degradation: Build full-featured first, add fallbacks for older browsers. PE is preferred — ensures core functionality is always available.

BWhat is the difference between a library and a framework?+

Library: You call it (React — you decide when/how to render). Framework: It calls you (Angular, Next.js — the framework controls the flow, you fill in the blanks). 'Inversion of control' is the key distinction. Libraries are flexible, frameworks are opinionated.

IWhat is a WebSocket?+

Full-duplex communication protocol over a single TCP connection. Unlike HTTP (request-response), WebSocket maintains a persistent connection for bidirectional data. Connection: HTTP upgrade request → WebSocket handshake. Use for: real-time chat, live data feeds, collaborative editing, gaming. Events: onopen, onmessage, onclose, onerror.

IWhat is a Progressive Web App (PWA)?+

Web app with native-like capabilities. Requirements: HTTPS, Service Worker (offline, push notifications, background sync), Web App Manifest (icon, name, splash screen, display mode). Features: installable on home screen, works offline, push notifications. manifest.json + navigator.serviceWorker.register(). Lighthouse audits PWA readiness.

IHow do you handle API errors in a frontend application?+

HTTP level: Check response.ok, handle status codes (401→login, 403→forbidden, 404→not found, 429→rate limited, 500→server error). UI: Error boundaries for render errors, toast notifications for transient errors, error pages for fatal errors. Retry: Exponential backoff for transient failures (TanStack Query does this automatically). Logging: Send errors to monitoring service (Sentry).

IWhat is optimistic UI?+

Update the UI immediately before server confirms the action. If server fails, revert. Makes the app feel instant. Example: liking a post — show the heart immediately, send request in background, revert if it fails. Implemented with: React 19's useOptimistic, TanStack Query's onMutate callback, Zustand with rollback.

IWhat is debouncing in the context of search?+

Wait until the user stops typing for N ms before sending API request. Without debouncing: each keystroke triggers a request (typing 'react' sends 5 requests). With debouncing (300ms delay): sends 1 request after the user pauses. Implementation: useDebounce hook or useDeferredValue in React.

IWhat is A/B testing architecture?+

Server-side: Middleware or edge function assigns variant (no layout shift). Client-side: Load variant after page load (can cause flicker). Implementation: Assign user to bucket (hash of user ID), store in cookie, serve variant. Metrics: Track conversion rate per variant. Tools: LaunchDarkly, Optimizely, GrowthBook, or custom with feature flags.

IWhat is image optimization strategy?+

Format: AVIF (best compression) > WebP > JPEG. Use <picture> for format fallbacks. Sizing: srcset with multiple widths, sizes attribute for viewport breakpoints. Loading: loading='lazy' for below-fold, fetchpriority='high' for hero. Placeholder: blur-up, LQIP, or solid color. CDN: Image CDN (Cloudflare Images, Imgix) for on-demand resizing. Next.js Image component handles most of this.

IHow do you implement infinite scroll?+

Trigger: Intersection Observer on a sentinel element at the bottom. Data: Cursor-based pagination (not offset-based — offset breaks with new items). Loading: TanStack Query's useInfiniteQuery manages pages, loading states, error recovery. Performance: Virtualize rendered items (@tanstack/react-virtual). UX: Loading spinner at bottom, 'load more' button as fallback, scroll restoration on back navigation.

ADesign a notification system.+

Delivery: WebSocket for real-time, Server-Sent Events for simpler setup, or polling as fallback. Types: In-app (toast/badge), push (Service Worker + Push API), email (server-side). State: Unread count (badge), notification list (paginated), read/unread toggle. Storage: Server database for history, client cache (TanStack Query). UX: Toast for new notifications, bell icon with unread count, notification center panel. Permission: Request push permission after user engagement (not on first visit).

ADesign a rich text editor (Medium-like).+

Foundation: contentEditable div (raw) or editor framework: ProseMirror (most flexible), TipTap (React-friendly wrapper), Lexical (Meta's, React-first), Slate.js. Data model: Document as AST (not HTML string) — enables operations, collaboration, validation. Features: Toolbar (bold, italic, headings, links), slash commands, image upload (presigned S3 URLs), embed (paste URL → preview). Collaboration: Yjs CRDT integration. Output: Serialize to HTML or custom format for rendering.

AHow do you handle feature flags in frontend?+

Architecture: Feature flag service (LaunchDarkly, Unleash, custom). Flags evaluated server-side (no flash of wrong content) or client-side (more flexible). Implementation: Context provider wrapping app, useFeatureFlag('new-checkout') hook. Server Components: Check flags in Server Components (no client JS for flag evaluation). Types: Boolean (on/off), percentage rollout, user targeting (beta users), A/B variants. Cleanup: Remove old flags regularly — feature flag debt is real.

ADesign a search experience (Google-like).+

Input: Debounced search input (300ms), autocomplete suggestions (separate API). Results: Server-rendered for SEO, paginated or infinite scroll, highlighted matching terms. Filters: URL search params for shareable/bookmarkable state. Performance: Search suggestions cached, results cached with SWR pattern. UX: Keyboard navigation in suggestions, recent searches (localStorage), no-results state with suggestions. Advanced: Faceted search, typo tolerance (server-side), search analytics.

AWhat are the trade-offs of SSR vs SSG vs ISR?+

SSG: Fastest delivery (CDN), can't personalize, stale until rebuild. Best: blog, docs, marketing. SSR: Always fresh, personalized, server load per request, slower TTFB. Best: dashboard, auth pages. ISR: CDN speed + periodic freshness, stale content possible for brief period. Best: e-commerce, CMS. PPR: Combines static shell with dynamic parts, best of all worlds but most complex. Choose based on: content freshness requirements, personalization needs, traffic volume.

AHow would you design an offline-first application?+

Service Worker: Cache-first strategy for static assets, network-first for API data. Storage: IndexedDB for structured data, Cache API for responses. Sync: Background Sync API for pending mutations when back online. Conflict resolution: Last-write-wins (simple) or CRDT (complex but correct). UX: Show cached data with 'offline' indicator, queue mutations with 'pending' state, sync indicator when back online. Libraries: Workbox for Service Worker, Dexie for IndexedDB.

ADesign an analytics dashboard.+

Data: Server Components fetch aggregated data (not raw events). Multiple parallel data fetches with Promise.all. Rendering: PPR — static layout/navigation, dynamic charts/metrics. Charts: Recharts, Chart.js, or D3 for complex visualizations. Lazy-load chart library. Interactivity: Date range picker, filters as URL params, drill-down navigation. Real-time: SSE or polling for live metrics. Performance: Virtual table for large datasets, skeleton loading for charts, memoize expensive calculations. Export: CSV/PDF generation via Server Actions.

UX/UI principles every frontend developer must know. Design decisions directly affect code architecture.

Core UX Principles

1. Heuristic Evaluation (Nielsen's 10 Heuristics)

Visibility of system status: Always show loading states, progress bars, success/error feedback. Never leave the user guessing.

Match between system and real world: Use familiar language, icons, and metaphors. A trash can icon for delete, not an X.

User control and freedom: Provide undo, redo, cancel, back. Let users recover from mistakes easily.

Consistency and standards: Same action = same result everywhere. Follow platform conventions (links are blue, buttons look clickable).

Error prevention: Disable submit until form is valid, confirm destructive actions, auto-save drafts.

Recognition over recall: Show options rather than making users remember. Autocomplete, recent items, breadcrumbs.

Flexibility and efficiency: Keyboard shortcuts for power users, simplified flows for beginners.

Aesthetic and minimalist design: Every extra element competes for attention. Remove what doesn't serve a purpose.

Help users recognize and recover from errors: Clear error messages with actionable solutions, not cryptic codes.

Help and documentation: Contextual tooltips, onboarding flows, searchable help.

These 10 heuristics are used in UX audits at companies like Google, Meta, and Amazon. Walk through each heuristic against your product to find issues. Interviewers often ask 'how would you improve the UX of X?' — use these as your framework.

Interaction Design Patterns

Loading States

Skeleton screens: Grey placeholder shapes mimicking content layout. Better than spinners because they show WHERE content will appear, reducing perceived load time by up to 50%.

Optimistic updates: Show the result immediately, sync in background. Like button turns red instantly — don't wait for server.

Progressive loading: Show content as it arrives (streaming SSR). User sees header + nav immediately, then content streams in.

Stale-while-revalidate: Show cached data immediately, refresh in background. TanStack Query does this automatically.

Feedback Patterns

Micro-interactions: Button press animation, toggle slide, checkbox bounce. Tiny details that make the UI feel alive.

Toast notifications: Temporary, non-blocking feedback. Auto-dismiss after 3-5s. Stack from bottom. Include undo for destructive actions.

Inline validation: Validate on blur (not keystroke). Show error below field. Green checkmark for valid. Don't validate empty fields until submit attempt.

Progress indicators: Determinate (progress bar with %) for known durations. Indeterminate (spinner) for unknown. Step indicators for multi-step flows.

The best loading state is no loading state. Use optimistic updates + skeleton screens + prefetching to make the app feel instant. Only show spinners for operations that truly take time (file uploads, complex calculations).

Responsive & Adaptive Design

Responsive Design Principles

Mobile-first: Design for smallest screen first, then enhance for larger screens. This forces you to prioritize content and simplify.

Breakpoints: Don't design for devices — design for content. Add a breakpoint when the layout breaks, not at iPhone/iPad sizes. Common: 640px (sm), 768px (md), 1024px (lg), 1280px (xl).

Touch targets: Minimum 44×44px (Apple) or 48×48px (Google). Fingers are imprecise — small buttons cause frustration.

Content priority: On mobile, stack vertically and hide secondary content behind toggles/accordions. Navigation → hamburger menu or bottom nav.

Adaptive Patterns

Container queries: Components adapt to their container, not viewport. A card in a sidebar looks different from the same card in main content.

Fluid typography: clamp(1rem, 2.5vw, 2rem) — scales smoothly without breakpoints.

Responsive images: srcset + sizes for resolution, <picture> for art direction (different crops per viewport).

Accessibility (a11y) for Developers

WCAG Levels

Level A: Minimum. Text alternatives for images, keyboard navigable, no seizure-causing content.

Level AA: Standard target. Color contrast 4.5:1 (text), 3:1 (large text), resize to 200% without loss, focus visible, error identification.

Level AAA: Enhanced. Contrast 7:1, no time limits, sign language for video. Rarely required but ideal.

Developer Checklist

Semantic HTML: Use <button> not <div onClick>. Use <nav>, <main>, <header>.

Keyboard navigation: Everything usable with Tab, Enter, Space, Escape, Arrow keys. Focus visible (never outline: none without replacement).

Color: Never use color alone to convey information. Add icons, text, or patterns alongside color.

Images: alt text for meaningful images, alt="" for decorative ones.

Forms: Labels linked to inputs, error messages associated with aria-describedby, fieldset/legend for groups.

Dynamic content: aria-live regions for updates, manage focus when content changes (modals, route transitions).

The #1 accessibility mistake: using <div> with onClick instead of <button>. A div has NO keyboard support, NO role, NO focus management. A button gets all of that for free. Use the right element and you get accessibility for free.

Design systems and design tokens — the bridge between design and engineering.

Design Tokens — Complete Guide

Design tokens are the atomic values of a design system — the single source of truth for colors, spacing, typography, shadows, borders, and animation.

Token Hierarchy

/* TIER 1: Global/Primitive tokens (raw values) */
--color-blue-500: #3b82f6;
--color-blue-600: #2563eb;
--color-gray-100: #f3f4f6;
--spacing-4: 1rem;
--spacing-8: 2rem;
--font-size-sm: 0.875rem;
--radius-md: 8px;

/* TIER 2: Semantic/Alias tokens (purpose-driven) */
--color-primary: var(--color-blue-600);
--color-text: var(--color-gray-900);
--color-text-muted: var(--color-gray-500);
--color-surface: var(--color-white);
--color-border: var(--color-gray-200);
--color-error: var(--color-red-600);
--color-success: var(--color-green-600);

/* TIER 3: Component tokens (specific usage) */
--button-bg: var(--color-primary);
--button-text: var(--color-white);
--button-radius: var(--radius-md);
--button-padding: var(--spacing-2) var(--spacing-4);
--card-bg: var(--color-surface);
--card-border: var(--color-border);
--card-shadow: 0 1px 3px rgb(0 0 0 / 0.1);
Think of tokens like a language translation chain: Global tokens are the dictionary (blue-500 = #3b82f6). Semantic tokens give meaning (primary = blue-600). Component tokens are sentences (button-bg = primary). Changing 'primary' in one place updates every component that uses it.

Theming with Tokens

/* Light theme (default) */
:root {{
  --color-bg: #ffffff;
  --color-text: #1f2937;
  --color-surface: #f9fafb;
  --color-primary: #3b82f6;
}}

/* Dark theme */
[data-theme="dark"] {{
  --color-bg: #0f172a;
  --color-text: #e2e8f0;
  --color-surface: #1e293b;
  --color-primary: #60a5fa;
}}

/* Auto from system preference */
@media (prefers-color-scheme: dark) {{
  :root:not([data-theme]) {{
    --color-bg: #0f172a;
    /* ... dark values */
  }}
}}

/* Components just use semantic tokens — theme-agnostic! */
.card {{
  background: var(--color-surface);
  color: var(--color-text);
  border: 1px solid var(--color-border);
}}

Design System Architecture

Component API Design

// Good component API — composable, predictable
<Button
  variant="primary" | "secondary" | "ghost" | "destructive"
  size="sm" | "md" | "lg"
  disabled={{boolean}}
  loading={{boolean}}
  leftIcon={{ReactNode}}
  asChild  // render as child element (Radix pattern)
>
  Label
</Button>

// Composable pattern (Radix/shadcn style)
<Dialog>
  <Dialog.Trigger asChild>
    <Button>Open</Button>
  </Dialog.Trigger>
  <Dialog.Content>
    <Dialog.Title>Confirm</Dialog.Title>
    <Dialog.Description>Are you sure?</Dialog.Description>
    <Dialog.Close />
  </Dialog.Content>
</Dialog>

Design System Tooling

Storybook: Component playground — each component has stories showing all variants, states, and edge cases. Enables visual testing, documentation, and design review.

Chromatic: Visual regression testing — screenshots every component on every PR, highlights visual diffs.

Figma tokens plugin: Sync design tokens between Figma and code. Designers change a color → code updates automatically.

Style Dictionary: Transform tokens from JSON into CSS variables, SCSS variables, iOS/Android constants, etc. One source file → every platform.

Typography System

/* Type scale — consistent rhythm */
:root {{
  --font-family-sans: 'Inter', system-ui, sans-serif;
  --font-family-mono: 'JetBrains Mono', monospace;
  --font-family-display: 'Cal Sans', sans-serif;
  
  /* Modular scale (1.25 ratio) */
  --text-xs: 0.75rem;    /* 12px */
  --text-sm: 0.875rem;   /* 14px */
  --text-base: 1rem;     /* 16px */
  --text-lg: 1.125rem;   /* 18px */
  --text-xl: 1.25rem;    /* 20px */
  --text-2xl: 1.5rem;    /* 24px */
  --text-3xl: 1.875rem;  /* 30px */
  --text-4xl: 2.25rem;   /* 36px */
  
  /* Line heights */
  --leading-tight: 1.2;   /* headings */
  --leading-normal: 1.6;  /* body text */
  --leading-relaxed: 1.8; /* long-form reading */
  
  /* Font weights */
  --font-normal: 400;
  --font-medium: 500;
  --font-semibold: 600;
  --font-bold: 700;
}}

/* Fluid typography — no breakpoints needed */
h1 {{ font-size: clamp(var(--text-2xl), 5vw, var(--text-4xl)); }}
Use a modular scale (ratio like 1.2 or 1.25) for font sizes. Each size is the previous × ratio. This creates natural visual harmony. Tools: type-scale.com or utopia.fyi for fluid scales.

Spacing & Layout System

/* 4px base spacing scale (Tailwind convention) */
:root {{
  --space-0: 0;
  --space-1: 0.25rem;  /* 4px */
  --space-2: 0.5rem;   /* 8px */
  --space-3: 0.75rem;  /* 12px */
  --space-4: 1rem;     /* 16px */
  --space-6: 1.5rem;   /* 24px */
  --space-8: 2rem;     /* 32px */
  --space-12: 3rem;    /* 48px */
  --space-16: 4rem;    /* 64px */
  --space-24: 6rem;    /* 96px */
}}

/* Usage — consistent spacing everywhere */
.card {{ padding: var(--space-6); gap: var(--space-4); }}
.section {{ margin-block: var(--space-16); }}
.stack > * + * {{ margin-block-start: var(--space-4); }}
A 4px grid means all spacing is a multiple of 4px. This creates visual rhythm and consistency — elements feel aligned and harmonious. When a designer says '16px padding,' you know that's var(--space-4). No magic numbers in your CSS.

Color System Design

Building a Color Palette

1. Brand colors: Primary (actions, links), secondary (accents), tertiary (highlights). Each needs 10 shades (50-950).

2. Neutral grays: 10 shades for text, backgrounds, borders. Slightly tinted with brand color for cohesion.

3. Semantic colors: Success (green), warning (yellow/amber), error/danger (red), info (blue). Each needs background + text + border variants.

4. Surface colors: Background, surface (card bg), elevated surface (modal bg), overlay.

Contrast & Accessibility

/* WCAG AA contrast ratios */
/* Normal text: 4.5:1 minimum */
/* Large text (18px+ or 14px+ bold): 3:1 minimum */
/* Non-text (icons, borders): 3:1 minimum */

/* Test with: Chrome DevTools contrast checker */
/* Or: contrast-ratio.com, polypane.app */

/* Common trap: gray text on white background */
color: #9ca3af; /* ❌ 2.9:1 on white — FAILS AA */
color: #6b7280; /* ✅ 4.6:1 on white — PASSES AA */
Don't just check contrast of your light theme. Dark themes need separate contrast checks — light text on dark backgrounds behaves differently. A color that passes in light mode might fail in dark mode and vice versa.

Motion & Animation Principles

When to Animate

DO animate: State changes (hover, focus, open/close), feedback (success/error), transitions between views, drawing attention to important changes.

DON'T animate: Just for decoration (distracting), critical content (users might miss it), anything that delays user tasks.

Animation Principles

Duration: 100-200ms for micro-interactions (hover, toggle). 200-500ms for medium transitions (modal open, page transition). Nothing over 1s.

Easing: ease-out for entering elements (decelerate into place). ease-in for exiting (accelerate out). ease-in-out for movement. Never linear for UI (feels robotic).

Direction: Content enters from where it came from and exits where it's going. A sidebar slides in from the left and out to the left.

Respect preferences: Always include @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) to disable animations for users who need it.

/* Standardized animation tokens */
:root {{
  --duration-fast: 100ms;
  --duration-normal: 200ms;
  --duration-slow: 400ms;
  --ease-default: cubic-bezier(0.4, 0, 0.2, 1);
  --ease-in: cubic-bezier(0.4, 0, 1, 1);
  --ease-out: cubic-bezier(0, 0, 0.2, 1);
  --ease-bounce: cubic-bezier(0.34, 1.56, 0.64, 1);
}}

@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) {{
  *, *::before, *::after {{
    animation-duration: 0.01ms !important;
    transition-duration: 0.01ms !important;
  }}
}}

Common UI patterns, form design, navigation, and real-world design decisions.

Form Design Best Practices

Layout

Single column: Faster to scan and complete than multi-column. Only use multi-column for short, related fields (first name / last name, city / state / zip).

Label placement: Above the field (fastest to scan), not to the left (wastes space, harder to scan). Placeholder text is NOT a label replacement — it disappears on focus.

Field grouping: Use <fieldset> + <legend> for related fields. Visual separation with spacing or subtle borders.

Validation

Validate on blur (when user leaves field), not on every keystroke (annoying). Show errors inline below the field, not in a summary at top. Use positive validation — green checkmark when valid, not just red when invalid.

Error messages: Specific and actionable. Not "Invalid input" but "Email must include @". Associate with aria-describedby for screen readers.

UX Details

Autocomplete: Set autocomplete attribute (email, name, address, tel, cc-number). Browsers auto-fill — massive UX win.

Input types: type="email" shows @ keyboard on mobile. type="tel" shows number pad. inputmode="numeric" for number-only fields.

Submit button: Disable when form is submitting (prevent double-submit). Show loading spinner. Primary action = most prominent button.

Navigation Patterns

Common Patterns

Top navigation bar: Logo left, primary nav center/right, auth/profile far right. Sticky on scroll. Responsive: hamburger menu on mobile.

Side navigation: For dashboards/admin panels. Collapsible (icon-only mode). Persistent across pages. Active state for current page.

Bottom navigation (mobile): 3-5 items max. Icons + labels. Active state. Used by: Instagram, YouTube, Twitter/X. Thumb-friendly zone.

Breadcrumbs: For deep hierarchies (e-commerce categories). Uses aria-label="Breadcrumb" + ol for semantics. Last item is current page (not a link).

Tabs: For switching content within same page. Use role="tablist", role="tab", role="tabpanel". Arrow key navigation between tabs. Only active tab is in tab order.

The 3-click rule is a myth — research shows users don't mind more clicks if each click is clear and confident. Focus on clear information scent (knowing where each click leads) rather than minimizing click count.

Dark Mode Implementation

/* Three-way toggle: light / dark / system */
/* 1. System preference (default) */
@media (prefers-color-scheme: dark) {{
  :root:not([data-theme]) {{ /* dark tokens */ }}
}}

/* 2. Manual override via data attribute */
[data-theme="light"] {{ /* light tokens */ }}
[data-theme="dark"] {{ /* dark tokens */ }}

/* 3. JavaScript for toggle + persistence */
// Read preference: localStorage.getItem('theme') || 'system'
// Apply: document.documentElement.dataset.theme = theme
// Persist: localStorage.setItem('theme', theme)

Dark Mode Design Tips

Don't just invert: Pure white (#fff) on pure black (#000) is too harsh. Use off-white (#e2e8f0) on dark gray (#0f172a).

Reduce saturation: Vibrant colors look harsh on dark backgrounds. Desaturate or use lighter variants.

Elevation with lightness: In light mode, shadows create depth. In dark mode, lighter surface colors create depth (no visible shadows on dark).

Check contrast separately: Colors that pass WCAG in light mode might fail in dark mode.

Micro-Interactions & Empty States

Micro-Interactions

Small animations that acknowledge user actions: button press (scale down 98%), toggle slide, checkbox bounce, ripple effect, hover lift (translateY -2px + shadow). They add personality and feel 'real.'

Empty States

Never show a blank page. When content is empty, show: 1) What this section is for. 2) Why it's empty. 3) A clear CTA to fix it.

Example: "No projects yet" + illustration + "Create your first project" button. Empty states are onboarding opportunities — guide the user to their first action.

Error States

404 page: Helpful message + search + link to home. Not just "Page not found." 500 page: Apologize + suggest retry + status page link. Offline state: Show cached content with "You're offline" banner + retry button.

28 interview questions from very basic to very advanced. Click to reveal answers.

Showing 28 questions
BWhat is UX vs UI?+

UX (User Experience): How the product feels — the entire journey. Includes research, information architecture, user flows, wireframes, usability testing. UI (User Interface): How it looks — visual design. Colors, typography, icons, spacing, animations. UX is the blueprint; UI is the paint and furnishing. Both are essential; UX without good UI feels clunky, UI without good UX is pretty but unusable.

BWhat are design tokens?+

Named design decisions stored as data: colors, spacing, typography, shadows, border radius, animation durations. They're the atomic values of a design system. Stored as CSS custom properties, JSON, or JS constants. Benefits: single source of truth, enable theming, sync between Figma and code, platform-agnostic (same tokens → CSS, iOS, Android).

BWhat is a design system?+

A collection of reusable components, design tokens, guidelines, and patterns that ensure consistency across products. Includes: 1) Design tokens (colors, spacing). 2) Component library (buttons, inputs, cards). 3) Documentation (usage guidelines, do's and don'ts). 4) Patterns (forms, navigation, data display). Examples: Material Design (Google), Carbon (IBM), Radix + shadcn/ui.

BWhat is visual hierarchy?+

Guiding the user's eye to the most important content first. Achieved through: Size (larger = more important), Color (high contrast = attention), Weight (bold text stands out), Spacing (whitespace isolates important elements), Position (top-left is read first in LTR), Depth (shadows/elevation). Primary CTA should be the most visually prominent element.

BWhat is whitespace (negative space)?+

Empty space between and around elements. NOT wasted space — it's a design tool. Benefits: improves readability, creates visual grouping (related items closer together), reduces cognitive load, feels premium/clean. Apple's design is famous for generous whitespace. Rule: when in doubt, add more space.

BWhat is the difference between wireframe, mockup, and prototype?+

Wireframe: Low-fidelity layout showing structure/content placement. No colors, real content, or interactivity. Focuses on information architecture. Mockup: High-fidelity static design with real colors, typography, images. Shows final visual appearance. Prototype: Interactive simulation of the product. Clickable flows, animations, real interactions. Tools: wireframe (Balsamiq), mockup/prototype (Figma).

BWhat is color contrast and why does it matter?+

The difference in luminance between foreground and background. WCAG AA requires 4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text. Low contrast = hard to read for everyone, impossible for visually impaired users. Test with Chrome DevTools (inspect element → color picker shows ratio). About 8% of men and 0.5% of women have color vision deficiency — never rely on color alone.

BWhat is the F-pattern?+

How users scan web pages: two horizontal sweeps at the top, then a vertical scan down the left side — forming an 'F'. Important content should be in: the first line (headline), the second line (subheadline/key info), and the left side of the page. This is why left-aligned text and top-loaded content work best.

BWhat is responsive design?+

Designing one product that works on all screen sizes. Principles: fluid layouts (%, fr, flex), media queries for breakpoints, responsive images (srcset), mobile-first approach (design for small screens first, enhance for larger). Modern: container queries (components adapt to their container), clamp() for fluid values, auto-fit/auto-fill grid columns.

BWhat is a CTA (Call to Action)?+

The element that prompts the user to take the desired action — sign up, buy, subscribe, start trial. Should be: visually prominent (contrasting color, large), clearly worded (action verb: 'Get Started' not 'Submit'), placed where users naturally look (above fold, end of content). One primary CTA per view — too many choices = no choice (Hick's Law).

IExplain Fitts's Law and its UX implications.+

The time to reach a target is a function of the distance to the target and the size of the target. Larger targets + closer placement = faster interaction. Implications: make primary buttons bigger, place frequent actions near cursor/thumb position, increase clickable area (padding on links/buttons), corners/edges of screen are 'infinite' targets (easy to reach). Mobile: bottom nav is faster than top nav (thumb zone).

IWhat is Hick's Law?+

Decision time increases with the number of choices. More options = longer to decide = higher chance of abandoning. Implications: limit nav items (5-7 max), progressive disclosure (show basic options first, advanced behind 'more'), categorize long lists, recommend a default option, reduce form fields to essentials. Amazon's 1-Click Buy is Hick's Law in action — zero decisions.

IWhat is Gestalt theory in design?+

How humans perceive visual groups: Proximity: Close items seem related. Similarity: Similar items seem grouped. Continuity: Eyes follow lines/curves. Closure: Brain completes incomplete shapes. Figure/Ground: Distinguishing foreground from background. Used in: card layouts (proximity), icon sets (similarity), progress indicators (continuity), logos (closure).

IHow do you implement dark mode properly?+

1) Token-based: All colors via CSS variables, swap token values per theme. 2) Three-way toggle: Light / Dark / System (respects prefers-color-scheme). 3) Persist choice: localStorage + cookie (for SSR). 4) Design adjustments: Lower saturation, use off-white not pure white, lighter surfaces = elevated, reduce shadows. 5) Separate contrast checks: Colors that pass in light may fail in dark. 6) Flash prevention: Set theme in script before body renders (inline blocking script).

IWhat is a component library vs design system?+

Component library: A collection of reusable UI components (buttons, inputs, modals). Code only. Design system: A component library PLUS: design tokens, usage guidelines, design principles, accessibility standards, interaction patterns, documentation, Figma components, and a governance process. A design system is a product that serves your product teams.

IWhat is the token hierarchy (primitive → semantic → component)?+

Primitive/Global: Raw values with no meaning: --blue-500: #3b82f6. Semantic/Alias: Purpose-driven names: --color-primary: var(--blue-600). Component: Usage-specific: --button-bg: var(--color-primary). This layering lets you: change the blue shade globally (primitive), change what 'primary' means (semantic), or customize one component (component) — without touching other layers.

IHow do you handle icon systems?+

SVG sprites: Define icons in a single SVG with <symbol> elements, reference with <use href='#icon-name'>. React icon components: Each icon is a component with props for size, color, title (accessibility). Icon fonts: Legacy approach (Font Awesome) — harder to style, poor accessibility. Modern: use SVG inline or sprite. Sizing: Token-based (16px, 20px, 24px). Color: currentColor inherits text color.

IWhat is progressive disclosure?+

Show only what's necessary at each step, revealing more as needed. Examples: 'Advanced settings' toggle, accordion FAQ, tooltip help text, multi-step wizard instead of one massive form, 'Show more' on long lists. Benefits: reduces cognitive load, focuses attention, doesn't overwhelm beginners while still serving power users.

IHow do you design accessible forms?+

Labels: Every input needs a visible <label> linked with for=. Errors: Inline below field, linked with aria-describedby, announced with aria-live. Required fields: required attribute + visual indicator (asterisk in label, not just color). Field groups: <fieldset> + <legend>. Keyboard: Tab order matches visual order, Enter submits. Autocomplete: Set autocomplete attributes for browser autofill.

IWhat are skeleton screens and when to use them?+

Grey placeholder shapes that mimic the layout of content being loaded. Better than spinners because they: set expectations for content layout, reduce perceived load time (users feel the page loaded faster), prevent layout shift (CLS). Use for: card lists, dashboards, profile pages — anything with a predictable layout. Don't use for: content with unpredictable layout, or very fast loads (<200ms).

IWhat is the difference between Material Design and Human Interface Guidelines?+

Material Design (Google): Paper metaphor, elevation with shadows, bold colors, ripple animations, 8dp grid, density levels. Platform: Android + web. HIG (Apple): Clarity (readable text, clear icons), deference (content first, chrome minimal), depth (layers, blur, translucency). Platform: iOS + macOS. Both are comprehensive but opinionated differently. Web apps often create their own design language drawing from both.

AHow do you build a theming system that supports multiple brands?+

Architecture: Three-layer tokens: global primitives → brand-specific semantic tokens → component tokens. Each brand overrides the semantic layer: [data-brand='acme'] maps --color-primary to their blue, while [data-brand='globex'] maps it to their purple. Components only reference semantic tokens. Build: Style Dictionary transforms one JSON source per brand into CSS. Runtime: Load brand CSS dynamically via media/data attribute. Testing: Storybook with brand selector addon for visual comparison.

AHow would you design and maintain a design system for a large organization?+

Team: Dedicated design system team (designers + engineers). Process: RFC for new components, design review + code review + a11y audit before merge. Versioning: Semver, changelog, deprecation warnings (one major version before removal). Documentation: Storybook with usage guidelines, do's/don'ts, API reference. Adoption: Codemods for breaking changes, migration guides, office hours for teams. Metrics: Track adoption rate, bug reports, satisfaction surveys. Governance: Clear contribution guidelines, regular audits, design/code token sync.

AExplain the concept of design tokens pipeline (from Figma to code).+

1) Figma: Designers define tokens using Figma Variables (colors, spacing, typography). 2) Export: Tokens plugin exports to JSON/YAML format. 3) Transform: Style Dictionary or Tokens Studio processes JSON into platform outputs: CSS variables, SCSS, JS/TS constants, iOS Swift, Android Kotlin. 4) Publish: Token package published to npm (or CDN). 5) Consume: Apps import tokens. 6) Sync: CI/CD triggers on token changes → auto-PR to consuming repos. Key: the JSON is the single source of truth, everything downstream is generated.

AHow do you measure and improve usability?+

Quantitative: Task success rate, time on task, error rate, SUS (System Usability Scale) score, NPS. Measured via analytics and A/B tests. Qualitative: Usability testing (5 users find 85% of issues), user interviews, heuristic evaluation (Nielsen's 10), cognitive walkthrough. Continuous: Session replay (Hotjar, FullStory), heatmaps, click maps, rage click detection. Process: Test early (wireframe testing), test often (every sprint), test with real users (not just team members). Iterate based on data, not opinions.

AHow do you handle design system versioning and breaking changes?+

Semver: Major (breaking), minor (new features), patch (fixes). Deprecation policy: Mark deprecated in current major, remove in next major (minimum 3 months). Visual deprecation warnings in Storybook and console. Codemods: Auto-transform old API to new: npx @myds/codemod button-v2. Migration guide: Step-by-step with before/after code. Canary releases: Let teams test before stable. Version dashboard: Track which teams are on which version.

AWhat is the Radix/shadcn approach and why has it become popular?+

Radix: Unstyled, accessible primitive components (Dialog, Dropdown, Tabs, etc.) with complete keyboard/ARIA support. You add your own styles. shadcn/ui: Copy-paste components built on Radix + Tailwind. Not a dependency — code lives in your project. Why popular: Full control over styling (no fighting a library's CSS), components are yours to modify, accessible by default, works with any styling approach. This 'headless UI + local components' pattern has replaced installed component libraries for many teams.

AHow do you ensure design-engineering consistency?+

Shared tokens: Designers and engineers use the SAME token values (Figma Variables synced to CSS variables). Component parity: Every Figma component has a code counterpart with matching props/variants. Design reviews: Engineers review designs for feasibility; designers review implementations for accuracy. Storybook: Living documentation that both teams reference. Visual regression: Chromatic catches unintended visual changes. Handoff: Figma Dev Mode provides exact specs, not PDF mocks.
